title: Respiratory System Vocabulary
description: Essential terminology for the respiratory system - airways, lungs, gas exchange, and breathing mechanics

questions.q: The process of gas exchange between the lungs and the blood is called:
questions.explain: External respiration is the gas exchange occurring at the respiratory membrane in the lungs - oxygen diffuses into pulmonary capillaries while CO2 diffuses out.

optionExplains.text: Ventilation is the mechanical process of moving air in and out (breathing), not gas exchange itself. Think: 'ventilation = air movement, respiration = gas exchange.'
Correct! External respiration happens at the alveolar-capillary membrane in the lungs. 'External' because it's at the body's interface with the outside air.
Internal respiration is gas exchange at the tissue level - between systemic capillaries and body cells. Same process, different location.
Cellular respiration is the metabolic process inside mitochondria that produces ATP - it's biochemistry, not gas exchange between compartments.

mechanism.title: Air-Blood Swap
mechanism.content: External respiration occurs when oxygen diffuses from alveolar air into pulmonary capillary blood while CO2 diffuses the opposite direction. This bidirectional exchange happens across the thin respiratory membrane driven by partial pressure gradients. The process refreshes blood with oxygen and removes metabolic waste CO2 before blood returns to the heart.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a loading dock where delivery trucks (blood) exchange full oxygen crates for empty CO2 containers, both vehicles arriving and leaving simultaneously through the same gate.

questions.q: The tiny air sacs where gas exchange occurs in the lungs are called:
questions.explain: Alveoli are microscopic air sacs at the ends of the respiratory tree with enormous surface area (~70 m²) for efficient gas exchange.

optionExplains.text: Bronchioles are small conducting airways that lead TO the alveoli - they're the 'hallways,' not the 'rooms' where exchange happens.
Bronchi are larger airways that branch from the trachea. They conduct air but are too thick-walled for gas exchange.
Correct! Alveoli are grape-like clusters of microscopic sacs where the respiratory membrane is thin enough (0.5 µm) for rapid diffusion.
Pleura are membranes that wrap around the lungs and line the chest cavity - they create the seal for negative pressure, not gas exchange.

mechanism.title: Microscopic Air Pockets
mechanism.content: Alveoli are grape-like sacs at the end of respiratory bronchioles with walls only one cell thick, creating minimal diffusion distance. Their enormous collective surface area (70 m2) surrounded by dense capillary networks maximizes gas exchange efficiency. Each alveolus is coated with surfactant to prevent collapse during exhalation.
mechanism.metaphor: Like bubble wrap where each tiny bubble is wrapped in blood vessels - millions of microscopic balloons creating a massive contact zone between air and blood in a compact space.

questions.q: The dome-shaped muscle that separates the thoracic and abdominal cavities is the:
questions.explain: The diaphragm is the primary muscle of inspiration, accounting for 75% of quiet breathing. When it contracts, it flattens and increases thoracic volume.

optionExplains.text: Intercostal muscles run between ribs and assist breathing, but they don't separate body cavities - they're accessory muscles, not the primary divider.
Correct! The diaphragm is a dome-shaped sheet that forms the floor of the thorax and roof of the abdomen. Contraction = inspiration.
Rectus abdominis is the 'six-pack' muscle of the anterior abdomen - it's for trunk flexion and forced expiration, not cavity separation.
Scalene muscles are neck muscles that elevate the upper ribs during forced inspiration - accessory muscles, not the thoraco-abdominal partition.

mechanism.title: Breathing Floor
mechanism.content: The diaphragm is a dome-shaped skeletal muscle separating the thoracic and abdominal cavities that contracts downward during inspiration. When it flattens, it increases vertical chest dimension and decreases intrathoracic pressure, causing air to rush into lungs. Relaxation allows passive exhalation as the dome returns upward.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a trampoline that pulls downward when stretched - when the diaphragm contracts and flattens, it creates suction that inflates the lungs like pulling a plunger on a syringe.

questions.q: The windpipe that conducts air from the larynx to the bronchi is the:
questions.explain: The trachea is reinforced by C-shaped cartilage rings to keep it open. The open part of the 'C' faces posteriorly against the esophagus.

optionExplains.text: The pharynx (throat) is above the larynx - it's a shared pathway for air and food before they diverge. Wrong direction in the airway sequence.
The esophagus is the food tube that runs behind the trachea - they're neighbors, but esophagus leads to stomach, not lungs.
Correct! The trachea is the 'windpipe' - a rigid tube from larynx (C6) to carina (T5) where it splits into main bronchi.
Bronchioles are tiny airways deep in the lungs, far downstream from where the trachea operates. Wrong size and wrong location.

mechanism.title: Central Air Tube
mechanism.content: The trachea is a 10-12 cm tube reinforced by C-shaped cartilage rings that prevent collapse during pressure changes of breathing. The posterior membranous wall allows esophageal expansion during swallowing. Ciliated pseudostratified epithelium with mucus-secreting goblet cells trap and sweep debris upward toward the pharynx.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a vacuum cleaner hose with rigid rings to keep it open - the rings prevent kinking while the soft back wall lets your esophagus expand when you swallow large bites.

questions.q: The voice box, containing the vocal cords, is called the:
questions.explain: The larynx houses the vocal cords (vocal folds) and controls airflow for speech. It sits at vertebral levels C3-C6.

optionExplains.text: The pharynx is the throat - a muscular tube above the larynx. It routes air to the larynx and food to the esophagus, but has no vocal cords.
Correct! The larynx ('voice box') contains the vocal folds that vibrate to produce sound. It's made of 9 cartilages including thyroid and cricoid.
The trachea is below the larynx - it's just a conducting tube with no vocal apparatus. Sound is already made before air reaches here.
The epiglottis is a single cartilage flap that covers the larynx during swallowing - it's part of the larynx, not the whole voice box.

mechanism.title: Sound Chamber
mechanism.content: The larynx houses the vocal cords (vocal folds) which vibrate when air passes through the glottis during exhalation. Pitch is controlled by tension of the cords via intrinsic laryngeal muscles, while volume depends on air pressure. The larynx also protects the airway by closing during swallowing.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a guitar where tightening strings (vocal cords) creates higher notes - air from your lungs strums the strings while muscles tune them, all inside a protective cartilage box.

questions.q: The membrane that lines the thoracic cavity wall is called the:
questions.explain: Parietal pleura lines the chest wall and diaphragm - 'parietal' means wall. The two pleural layers create a potential space with negative pressure.

optionExplains.text: Visceral pleura covers the lungs themselves (viscera = organs). It's the 'inner layer' that moves with the lung, not the wall lining.
Correct! Parietal pleura = 'wall pleura' - it lines the inside of the chest wall, mediastinum, and diaphragm. Parietal always means 'wall.'
Pericardium is the membrane surrounding the heart, not the thoracic wall. Different organ, similar double-layer structure.
Peritoneum lines the abdominal cavity, not the thoracic cavity. 'Peri-' means around, but different body compartment.

mechanism.title: Wall Lining
mechanism.content: The parietal pleura is the outer layer of the pleural sac that adheres to the thoracic wall, diaphragm, and mediastinum. It is continuous with the visceral pleura at the lung root (hilum), creating a sealed potential space containing a thin film of pleural fluid. This allows frictionless lung movement during breathing while coupling lung expansion to chest wall movement.
mechanism.metaphor: Like the inside of a wet plastic bag pressed against your ribs - the bag's outer layer (parietal) clings to your chest wall while the inner layer glides smoothly against your lungs with every breath.

questions.q: The substance that reduces surface tension in the alveoli is called:
questions.explain: Surfactant is produced by type II alveolar cells and prevents alveolar collapse by reducing surface tension at the air-liquid interface.

optionExplains.text: Mucus traps particles in upper airways but doesn't reduce surface tension in alveoli. Wrong substance, wrong location.
Correct! Surfactant is a phospholipid mixture (mainly DPPC) that prevents alveolar collapse during expiration. Premature infants lack it = respiratory distress syndrome.
Serous fluid lubricates pleural surfaces, not alveoli. It's in the pleural space between parietal and visceral layers.
Plasma is blood fluid - it doesn't contact alveolar air. Gas exchange happens across the respiratory membrane, but plasma stays in capillaries.

mechanism.title: Bubble Stabilizer
mechanism.content: Surfactant is a phospholipid mixture secreted by type II alveolar cells that reduces surface tension at the air-liquid interface of alveoli. By lowering surface tension, it prevents alveolar collapse during exhalation and reduces the work of breathing. Surfactant also helps equalize pressure across alveoli of different sizes, preventing smaller alveoli from emptying into larger ones.
mechanism.metaphor: Like dish soap in bubble solution - without it, tiny bubbles pop immediately, but with surfactant, even microscopic alveolar bubbles stay inflated with minimal effort between breaths.

questions.q: The volume of air moved in a normal, quiet breath is called:
questions.explain: Tidal volume averages about 500 mL in adults during normal breathing - like the tide going in and out, it's the regular rhythm.

optionExplains.text: Residual volume is air that stays in lungs after maximum expiration (~1200 mL). It never moves - that's why it's 'residual.'
Vital capacity is the MAXIMUM air you can move (IRV + TV + ERV), not normal breathing. It's used in pulmonary function tests.
Correct! Tidal volume = ~500 mL per breath at rest. 'Tidal' like ocean tides - the regular, rhythmic in-and-out of quiet breathing.
Inspiratory reserve is EXTRA air you CAN inhale beyond tidal volume (~3000 mL). It's a reserve capacity, not normal breathing.

mechanism.title: Resting Breath Size
mechanism.content: Tidal volume is the amount of air (approximately 500 mL) that moves in or out of the lungs during normal quiet breathing. It represents only a fraction of total lung capacity, leaving substantial inspiratory and expiratory reserve volumes available. Tidal volume can increase dramatically during exercise as metabolic oxygen demand rises.
mechanism.metaphor: Like sipping from a glass instead of chugging - you only use a small fraction of your lung capacity with each normal breath, keeping plenty in reserve for when you suddenly need to sprint.

questions.q: The point where the trachea divides into the two main bronchi is called the:
questions.explain: The carina is the cartilaginous ridge at the tracheal bifurcation at vertebral level T5 (sternal angle level).

optionExplains.text: The hilum is where bronchi, vessels, and nerves enter each lung - it's ON the lung, not in the trachea. Close but different landmark.
Correct! The carina (Latin for 'keel') is the ridge where the trachea splits into right and left main bronchi. Highly sensitive to foreign objects.
The sternal angle is a bony landmark on the sternum at T4-T5 level - it's external and helps locate the carina, but isn't the carina itself.
The cricoid is a cartilage ring of the larynx, way above the tracheal bifurcation. It's at C6, carina is at T5.

mechanism.title: Airway Split Point
mechanism.content: The carina is the internal ridge where the trachea bifurcates into right and left main bronchi, located at the level of the sternal angle (T4-T5). It contains highly sensitive mucosa that triggers violent coughing when irritated, protecting against aspiration. The right main bronchus is wider and more vertical, making it the more common site for foreign body aspiration.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a wishbone dividing a single tube into two branches - touch this sensitive ridge with food or a medical instrument and you'll trigger an explosive cough reflex to protect your lungs.

questions.q: Difficult or labored breathing is medically termed:
questions.explain: Dyspnea is the subjective sensation of breathing discomfort or 'air hunger.' Dys- = difficult, -pnea = breathing.

optionExplains.text: Apnea means NO breathing (a- = without). It's cessation of breathing, not difficult breathing. Think: sleep apnea = breathing stops.
Correct! Dyspnea = difficult breathing. It's subjective (patient feels it) and can occur with lung disease, heart failure, anxiety, or exercise.
Eupnea is NORMAL, comfortable breathing (eu- = good/normal). It's the opposite of dyspnea - easy, unlabored breathing.
Hyperpnea is increased DEPTH of breathing (hyper- = excessive). It can be normal during exercise and isn't necessarily uncomfortable.

mechanism.title: Air Hunger Sensation
mechanism.content: Dyspnea is the subjective sensation of difficult or labored breathing, often described as shortness of breath or air hunger. It arises from mismatches between respiratory effort and ventilatory output, mediated by mechanoreceptors in airways and chemoreceptors detecting blood gas imbalances. Dyspnea can occur without hypoxia in conditions affecting respiratory mechanics or perception.
mechanism.metaphor: Like trying to breathe through a coffee stirrer while running - your brain screams for more air but your respiratory system can't deliver it fast enough, creating a desperate sensation even if oxygen levels are adequate.

questions.q: The flap of cartilage that covers the larynx during swallowing is the:
questions.explain: The epiglottis folds down like a trap door to protect the airway during swallowing, preventing food from entering the trachea.

optionExplains.text: Thyroid cartilage is the 'Adam's apple' - a large shield-shaped cartilage that protects the larynx but doesn't move during swallowing.
Cricoid cartilage is a complete ring below the thyroid cartilage - it provides structural support but doesn't cover the airway.
Correct! The epiglottis is a leaf-shaped flap that tips backward during swallowing to cover the glottis and direct food to the esophagus.
Arytenoid cartilages are paired pyramids that move the vocal cords - they're for voice production, not airway protection.

mechanism.title: Swallowing Trapdoor
mechanism.content: The epiglottis is a leaf-shaped elastic cartilage that passively covers the laryngeal inlet during swallowing, redirecting food and liquid into the esophagus. The upward laryngeal movement during swallowing tips the epiglottis posteriorly like a hinged lid. This prevents aspiration while allowing normal breathing when the larynx is at rest.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a one-way drain cover that flips closed when water (food) flows - when you swallow, your larynx rises and tips this flexible lid backward to seal your windpipe shut.

questions.q: The protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen is:
questions.explain: Hemoglobin has four heme groups, each binding one oxygen molecule. Each RBC contains ~280 million hemoglobin molecules.

optionExplains.text: Myoglobin stores oxygen in MUSCLE cells, not blood. It has only one heme group (hemoglobin has four). Think: myo = muscle.
Correct! Hemoglobin (Hb) is in red blood cells - 4 subunits, 4 heme groups, carries up to 4 O2 molecules. 'Heme' = iron-containing.
Albumin is a plasma protein that maintains oncotic pressure and transports substances, but it doesn't carry oxygen.
Fibrinogen is a clotting factor - it converts to fibrin during coagulation. No oxygen-carrying role.

mechanism.title: Iron Oxygen Courier
mechanism.content: Hemoglobin is a quaternary protein in red blood cells with four heme groups, each containing an iron atom that reversibly binds one oxygen molecule. Cooperative binding causes oxygen affinity to increase as more O2 binds (sigmoidal saturation curve). Hemoglobin also transports about 20% of CO2 and buffers blood pH through its amino acid side chains.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a four-person taxi with iron seats - when the first passenger (oxygen) gets in, it becomes easier for the next three to hop in, and when they all exit at tissues, the empty cab picks up CO2 for the return trip.

questions.q: The respiratory centers that control breathing rate are located in the:
questions.explain: The medulla contains the VRG (ventral respiratory group) and DRG (dorsal respiratory group); the pons has the pneumotaxic center.

optionExplains.text: Cerebral cortex allows VOLUNTARY breath control (holding breath, sighing), but automatic breathing doesn't depend on it.
Cerebellum coordinates movement and balance - it has no respiratory control centers.
Correct! The brainstem (medulla + pons) controls automatic breathing. Medulla sets the rhythm; pons modulates rate and depth.
Hypothalamus regulates body temperature, hunger, and hormones - not breathing rate directly.

mechanism.title: Automatic Breath Control
mechanism.content: Respiratory centers in the medulla oblongata and pons automatically generate rhythmic breathing by controlling diaphragm and intercostal muscle contraction. The medullary rhythmicity center sets the basic breathing rate, while pontine centers (pneumotaxic and apneustic) modulate rhythm smoothness. Central chemoreceptors respond to CSF pH changes from CO2, while peripheral chemoreceptors detect blood O2, CO2, and pH.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a conductor (medulla) keeping orchestra tempo while assistant directors (pons) adjust phrasing - the conductor reads sheet music (blood chemistry) and automatically cues musicians (respiratory muscles) without conscious thought.

questions.q: The condition of collapsed lung due to air in the pleural cavity is called:
questions.explain: Pneumothorax occurs when air enters the pleural space, breaking the negative pressure seal and causing lung collapse.

optionExplains.text: Atelectasis is lung collapse from AIRWAY obstruction (mucus plug, tumor) - no air in pleural space. Different mechanism.
Correct! Pneumo- = air, -thorax = chest. Air in pleural cavity breaks the vacuum, and the lung recoils inward. Can be traumatic or spontaneous.
Pleurisy is inflammation of the pleura causing painful breathing - no air accumulation or collapse involved.
Emphysema is destruction of alveolar walls (COPD) - lungs are overinflated, not collapsed. Chronic, not acute.

mechanism.title: Collapsed Lung Air
mechanism.content: Pneumothorax occurs when air enters the pleural space, breaking the negative pressure that keeps lungs inflated against the chest wall. The affected lung collapses toward the hilum due to its natural elastic recoil as the pressure gradient is lost. Tension pneumothorax occurs when a one-way valve effect allows air to accumulate with each breath, progressively compressing mediastinal structures.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a vacuum-sealed bag of coffee that gets punctured - once air enters the sealed space, the compressed contents (lung) spring away from the walls and the packaging loses its shape.

questions.q: The exchange of gases between blood and tissue cells is called:
questions.explain: Internal respiration is gas exchange at the systemic capillary level - oxygen leaves blood, CO2 enters blood at tissues.

optionExplains.text: External respiration happens in the LUNGS (gas exchange at alveoli). 'External' because it's at the body's interface with outside air.
Correct! Internal respiration = gas exchange at TISSUES. O2 diffuses from capillaries to cells; CO2 diffuses from cells to capillaries.
Pulmonary ventilation is just breathing (moving air in/out) - it's mechanical, not gas exchange.
Cellular respiration is ATP production inside cells (mitochondria) - biochemistry, not gas exchange between compartments.

mechanism.title: Tissue Oxygen Drop
mechanism.content: Internal respiration is the exchange of gases between systemic capillary blood and tissue cells throughout the body. Oxygen diffuses from blood into metabolically active cells along its concentration gradient, while CO2 produced by cellular respiration diffuses into blood. This process sustains cellular ATP production and removes acidic waste products.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a delivery truck dropping off oxygen groceries at every house (cell) on its route while picking up trash bags (CO2) at each stop - the same principle as lung exchange but reversed.

questions.q: The nasal cavity is divided into two halves by the:
questions.explain: The nasal septum is made of bone (perpendicular plate of ethmoid + vomer) anteriorly and cartilage posteriorly.

optionExplains.text: Conchae (same as turbinates) are shelf-like projections from the lateral walls that increase surface area - they don't divide the cavity.
Correct! The nasal septum is the midline partition creating left and right nasal passages. Deviated septum = crooked divider.
Turbinates (conchae) project from side walls to warm/humidify air - they're inside each half, not the divider.
Sinuses are air-filled cavities in facial bones that drain INTO the nasal cavity - they're accessory, not the divider.

mechanism.title: Nose Dividing Wall
mechanism.content: The nasal septum is a midline partition of bone (perpendicular plate of ethmoid and vomer) and cartilage that divides the nasal cavity into right and left chambers. It provides structural support to the external nose and creates two parallel airflow channels. Deviation of the septum can obstruct airflow and impair nasal breathing.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a vertical diving board in a swimming pool - the septum splits one nose into two tunnels, keeping left and right airflows separate while supporting the bridge of your nose.

questions.q: The normal oxygen saturation of arterial blood is approximately:
questions.explain: Normal arterial oxygen saturation (SaO2) is 95-100%. Below 90% indicates hypoxemia requiring intervention.

optionExplains.text: 70-75% is normal for VENOUS blood (returning from tissues) - arterial should be much higher after lung oxygenation.
85-90% is borderline hypoxemia - concerning but not crisis. You'd investigate but not necessarily give emergent oxygen.
Correct! 95-100% is normal SpO2. Pulse oximeters measure this. <94% is hypoxemia; <90% is serious; <80% is severe.
50-60% would be life-threatening hypoxemia - this is cardiac arrest territory, not normal values.

mechanism.title: Blood Oxygen Fullness
mechanism.content: Oxygen saturation (SpO2) is the percentage of hemoglobin binding sites occupied by oxygen, normally 95-100% in arterial blood. It reflects the balance between oxygen loading in lungs and oxygen consumption by tissues. Saturation is measured noninvasively by pulse oximetry, which uses differential light absorption by oxygenated vs deoxygenated hemoglobin.
mechanism.metaphor: Like parking lot fullness - if 98 of 100 parking spots (hemoglobin sites) are filled with cars (oxygen molecules), your lot is 98% saturated, indicating healthy oxygen delivery capacity.

options: Alveolar wall, basement membranes, and capillary wall
questions.explain: The respiratory membrane is about 0.5 micrometers thick: type I alveolar cell + fused basement membranes + capillary endothelium.

optionExplains.text: Close but incomplete - there are actually basement membranes between the alveolar and capillary walls that are part of the barrier.
Correct! Three layers: (1) alveolar epithelium, (2) fused basement membranes, (3) capillary endothelium. Total ~0.5 um for fast diffusion.
Bronchioles have smooth muscle but are CONDUCTING airways - too thick for gas exchange. Wrong location entirely.
Pleural membranes are around the lung, not in alveoli. They create the seal for breathing mechanics, not gas exchange.

mechanism.title: Three-Layer Barrier
mechanism.content: The respiratory membrane consists of the alveolar wall (simple squamous epithelium), fused basement membranes, and the capillary wall (endothelium). This three-layer structure is only 0.5 micrometers thick, minimizing diffusion distance for gases. Oxygen and CO2 cross all three layers rapidly following their partial pressure gradients.
mechanism.metaphor: Like three sheets of tissue paper pressed together - air on one side, blood on the other, and only three impossibly thin layers between them allowing oxygen and CO2 to pass through almost instantly.

questions.q: The process of breathing air into the lungs is called:
questions.explain: Inspiration is the active phase where diaphragm and intercostals contract, expanding the thorax and drawing air in.

optionExplains.text: Expiration is breathing OUT - air leaves the lungs. It's mostly passive during quiet breathing (elastic recoil).
Correct! Inspiration = breathing IN. 'In-spire' = breathe in. Active process requiring muscle contraction to expand thoracic volume.
Respiration is the broader term for gas exchange (external + internal) - inspiration is just the inhalation part of ventilation.
Ventilation includes BOTH inspiration AND expiration - the complete cycle of breathing, not just inhalation.

mechanism.title: Active Chest Expansion
mechanism.content: Inspiration is the active phase of breathing where diaphragm contraction and external intercostal muscle contraction expand thoracic volume. This expansion decreases intrathoracic pressure below atmospheric pressure, creating a pressure gradient that drives air into the lungs. Quiet inspiration requires muscle work, while exhalation is typically passive elastic recoil.
mechanism.metaphor: Like pulling the plunger on a syringe - your muscles actively expand your chest cavity, creating suction that pulls air in through your nose and mouth until pressures equalize.

questions.q: Carbon dioxide is primarily transported in the blood as:
questions.explain: About 70% of CO2 is converted to bicarbonate ions by carbonic anhydrase in RBCs. This also helps buffer blood pH.

optionExplains.text: Only ~7% of CO2 dissolves directly in plasma - it's not very soluble, so this is a minor transport mechanism.
~23% binds to hemoglobin (as carbaminohemoglobin) - significant but not the PRIMARY method.
Correct! ~70% is transported as HCO3-. CO2 + H2O -> H2CO3 -> H+ + HCO3- (catalyzed by carbonic anhydrase in RBCs).
Carbonic acid (H2CO3) is an intermediate that immediately dissociates to H+ and HCO3- - it doesn't accumulate.

mechanism.title: Chemical CO2 Shuttle
mechanism.content: About 70% of CO2 is transported in blood as bicarbonate ions formed when CO2 combines with water catalyzed by carbonic anhydrase in red blood cells. This reaction (CO2 + H2O - H2CO3 - H+ + HCO3-) is reversed in lung capillaries, releasing CO2 for exhalation. Bicarbonate transport also serves as the major blood pH buffer system.
mechanism.metaphor: Like dissolving sugar in coffee for transport - CO2 can't ride freely in blood plasma, so it dissolves into bicarbonate solution for the journey, then crystallizes back into gas when it reaches the lungs.

questions.q: The right lung has how many lobes?
questions.explain: The right lung has three lobes (superior, middle, inferior) separated by horizontal and oblique fissures. The left has only two.

optionExplains.text: Two lobes is for the LEFT lung - it has only superior and inferior lobes because the heart takes up space (cardiac notch).
Correct! Right lung = 3 lobes (superior, middle, inferior). It's larger than left because heart indents the left side.
No lung has four lobes. Three is the maximum (right lung). Easy to remember: Right = 3 letters = 3 lobes.
Five lobes total (3 right + 2 left), but the question asks about the RIGHT lung specifically.

mechanism.title: Right Lung Tripartite
mechanism.content: The right lung is divided into superior, middle, and inferior lobes by the horizontal and oblique fissures, while the left lung has only superior and inferior lobes. This asymmetry exists because the heart occupies more space on the left side of the thorax. The right lung is also slightly larger than the left, contributing about 55% of total lung capacity.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a bookshelf where the right side has three shelves and the left has two - the heart acts like a bulky object on the left, leaving room for only two compartments instead of three.

questions.q: The area in the lungs where bronchi, vessels, and nerves enter is called the:
questions.explain: The hilum is the 'root' of the lung on its medial surface - bronchi, pulmonary vessels, lymphatics, and nerves enter here.

optionExplains.text: The apex is the pointed TOP of the lung that extends above the clavicle - no vessels enter here.
The base is the BOTTOM of the lung resting on the diaphragm - vessels don't enter from below.
Correct! The hilum (Latin for 'little thing') is on the mediastinal surface where the 'root' structures enter/exit the lung.
The cardiac notch is an indentation on the LEFT lung where the heart sits - it's not an entry point for structures.

mechanism.title: Lung Root Gateway
mechanism.content: The hilum is the medial depression on each lung where the bronchi, pulmonary vessels, lymphatics, and nerves enter and exit, collectively forming the lung root. This is the only site where the visceral and parietal pleura are continuous. The hilum serves as the structural and vascular pedicle anchoring the lung within the thorax.
mechanism.metaphor: Like the base of a tree trunk where all the roots meet - all the tubes (airways, blood vessels, nerves) that keep the lung alive bundle together at this single entry/exit point.

questions.q: The volume of air remaining after forced expiration is called:
questions.explain: Residual volume (~1200 mL) cannot be exhaled - it keeps alveoli partially inflated and prevents collapse.

optionExplains.text: Tidal volume is normal breathing (~500 mL) - it moves in AND out, not what stays behind.
Expiratory reserve is extra air you CAN forcefully exhale beyond tidal (~1200 mL) - it's not what remains.
Correct! Residual volume = air that CANNOT be exhaled even with maximum effort. It keeps airways from collapsing completely.
Vital capacity is MAXIMUM air you can move (IRV + TV + ERV) - it doesn't include residual volume.

mechanism.title: Trapped Reserve Air
mechanism.content: Residual volume (approximately 1200 mL) is the air remaining in lungs after maximal forced exhalation that cannot be voluntarily expelled. It keeps alveoli from collapsing completely and maintains some gas exchange even between breaths. Residual volume increases with age and in obstructive lung diseases as elastic recoil diminishes.
mechanism.metaphor: Like the last bit of toothpaste you can never squeeze out - no matter how hard you exhale, your lungs keep about a liter of air trapped inside to prevent the alveoli from sticking shut.

questions.q: The mucus-secreting cells lining the respiratory tract are called:
questions.explain: Goblet cells produce sticky mucus that traps inhaled particles. Cilia then sweep the mucus upward (mucociliary escalator).

optionExplains.text: Ciliated cells have hair-like projections that MOVE mucus, but they don't MAKE mucus. They're partners with goblet cells.
Correct! Goblet cells are named for their goblet (wine glass) shape. They secrete mucin that hydrates into sticky mucus.
Type I alveolar cells are thin squamous cells for GAS EXCHANGE - they're in alveoli, not conducting airways.
Type II alveolar cells produce SURFACTANT, not mucus. They're also in alveoli, not the upper airways.

mechanism.title: Mucus Factory Cells
mechanism.content: Goblet cells are specialized columnar epithelial cells scattered throughout respiratory passages that synthesize and secrete mucus glycoproteins. Their mucus traps inhaled particles, pathogens, and debris, which are then swept toward the pharynx by ciliary action (mucociliary escalator). Goblet cell numbers increase in chronic inflammatory conditions, contributing to excessive mucus production.
mechanism.metaphor: Like automated glue dispensers on an assembly line - these cells continuously pump out sticky mucus that acts as flypaper, trapping dust and bacteria before beating cilia sweep the whole mess upward to be swallowed or coughed out.

questions.q: The law stating that gas diffuses from high to low pressure is:
questions.explain: Fick's law: diffusion rate = (Area x Gradient x Solubility) / Thickness. Gases always move down their pressure gradient.

optionExplains.text: Henry's law: gas solubility in liquid is proportional to partial pressure. It's about dissolving, not diffusion direction.
Boyle's law: P1V1 = P2V2 (pressure-volume relationship). It explains lung volume changes, not gas diffusion.
Dalton's law: total pressure = sum of partial pressures. It's about gas mixtures, not diffusion direction.
Correct! Fick's law describes diffusion: gases move from high to low partial pressure, rate depends on area, thickness, and solubility.

mechanism.title: Diffusion Rate Law
mechanism.content: Fick's law states that the rate of gas diffusion across a membrane is proportional to surface area and partial pressure difference, and inversely proportional to membrane thickness and molecular weight. The respiratory membrane is optimized for rapid diffusion with enormous surface area (70 m2), minimal thickness (0.5 um), and steep pressure gradients. Diseases that thicken the membrane or reduce surface area impair gas exchange.
mechanism.metaphor: Like water seeping through a paper towel faster when it's thin, wet, and has lots of area - gases cross your lung membrane quickest when the pressure difference is steep, the barrier is thin, and the contact zone is huge.

questions.explain: Pneumo- comes from Greek 'pneuma' meaning breath/air. Examples: pneumonia, pneumothorax, pneumonectomy.

optionExplains.text: Heart = cardio- (Greek kardia). Pneumo- is respiratory, not cardiovascular.
Correct! Pneumo- = lung/air. Pneumonia = lung infection. Pneumothorax = air in chest. PNEUMatic = air-powered.
Blood = hemo-/hemat- (Greek haima). Pneumo- doesn't relate to blood.
Muscle = myo- (Greek mys). Pneumo- is about air/lungs, not muscles.

mechanism.title: Lung Air Prefix
mechanism.content: The prefix pneumo- derives from Greek 'pneuma' meaning air, breath, or lung and appears in medical terms related to the respiratory system. Examples include pneumonia (lung inflammation), pneumothorax (air in pleural cavity), and pneumonectomy (lung removal). It distinguishes respiratory conditions from similar terms using 'pulmo-' (Latin for lung).
mechanism.metaphor: Like how 'aqua-' signals water in words - when you see 'pneumo-', think lungs and air, whether it's pneumonia (lung infection), pneumothorax (collapsed lung), or pneumococcus (a lung bacterium).

questions.explain: Broncho- relates to the bronchi, the branching airways. Examples: bronchitis, bronchoscopy, bronchodilator.

optionExplains.text: Nose = rhino- (Greek rhis). That's why nose surgery is 'rhinoplasty.'
Throat = pharyngo- (pharynx) or laryngo- (larynx). Bronchi are below the throat.
Correct! Broncho- = bronchi (airways). Bronchitis = inflamed bronchi. Bronchodilator = opens airways.
Lungs as a whole = pulmo- or pneumo-. Bronchi are the AIRWAYS within lungs, not the lung tissue itself.

mechanism.title: Airway Tree Prefix
mechanism.content: The prefix broncho- refers to the bronchi and bronchial tree, the branching airways that conduct air from trachea to alveoli. Terms include bronchitis (airway inflammation), bronchodilator (medication that widens airways), and bronchoscopy (visual examination of airways). It distinguishes airway-specific conditions from general lung or alveolar pathology.
mechanism.metaphor: Like 'cardio-' points to the heart - 'broncho-' points specifically to the airway tubes, whether you're dilating them (bronchodilator), inflaming them (bronchitis), or scoping them (bronchoscopy).

questions.explain: -pnea comes from Greek meaning breathing. Common terms: dyspnea, apnea, tachypnea, bradypnea, orthopnea.

optionExplains.text: Chest = -thorax (as in pneumothorax). Different suffix.
Correct! -pnea = breathing. Dys-pnea = difficult breathing. A-pnea = no breathing. Tachy-pnea = fast breathing.
Lung = pneumo- or pulmo- (as prefix). -pnea specifically means the ACT of breathing.
Air = pneumo- (as prefix). -pnea is about the breathing process, not air itself.

mechanism.title: Breathing Suffix
mechanism.content: The suffix -pnea means breathing or respiration, derived from Greek 'pnoia.' It combines with prefixes to describe breathing patterns: dyspnea (difficult), tachypnea (rapid), bradypnea (slow), apnea (absent), orthopnea (difficulty when lying flat). The suffix allows precise clinical description of respiratory abnormalities.
mechanism.metaphor: Like '-ology' means study of - '-pnea' means breathing, so you can decode medical terms: tachy-pnea is fast-breathing, brady-pnea is slow-breathing, and a-pnea is no-breathing.

questions.explain: Hypoxia is inadequate oxygen delivery to tissues. Hypo- = below/deficient, -oxia = oxygen condition.

optionExplains.text: Excess oxygen would be hyperoxia (hyper- = excessive). Hypoxia is the opposite - too LITTLE oxygen.
Correct! Hypoxia = low tissue oxygen. Causes: lung disease, anemia, poor circulation, high altitude. Can damage organs.
Normal oxygen would be normoxia or just... normal. Hypoxia specifically means deficiency.
Absence of breathing is apnea (a- = without, -pnea = breathing). Hypoxia is about oxygen level, not breathing.

mechanism.title: Tissue Oxygen Starvation
mechanism.content: Hypoxia is inadequate oxygen delivery to tissues despite potentially normal blood oxygen levels, caused by low oxygen content, poor perfusion, or impaired cellular uptake. Cells switch to less efficient anaerobic metabolism, producing lactate and triggering compensatory responses like increased ventilation and heart rate. Severe hypoxia causes organ dysfunction and cell death.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a town where grocery delivery trucks (blood) might be full but roads are blocked - tissues starve for oxygen even if your lungs are working fine, because the oxygen can't reach cells that desperately need it.

questions.explain: Hypercapnia = elevated blood CO2. Hyper- = excessive, -capnia = CO2. Often caused by hypoventilation.

optionExplains.text: Low CO2 is hypocapnia (hypo- = below). Usually from hyperventilation blowing off too much CO2.
Correct! Hypercapnia = high CO2 (>45 mmHg). Causes respiratory acidosis. Seen in COPD, respiratory failure.
High oxygen is hyperoxia. -capnia specifically refers to CO2, not oxygen.
Low blood pH is acidosis (or acidemia). Hypercapnia CAUSES acidosis, but they're not the same thing.

mechanism.title: CO2 Blood Buildup
mechanism.content: Hypercapnia is elevated arterial CO2 (PaCO2 > 45 mmHg) resulting from inadequate alveolar ventilation relative to CO2 production. It causes respiratory acidosis as CO2 combines with water to form carbonic acid, lowering blood pH. Symptoms include headache, confusion, and dyspnea, while severe hypercapnia can cause CO2 narcosis and respiratory failure.
mechanism.metaphor: Like trash piling up when garbage trucks stop running - your blood becomes a toxic dump of CO2 waste when breathing can't keep pace with cellular production, poisoning your system with acid.

questions.explain: Cyanosis appears when deoxygenated hemoglobin exceeds 5 g/dL, giving a bluish tint to skin and mucous membranes.

optionExplains.text: Redness (erythema) is from INCREASED blood flow or oxygenation - opposite of cyanosis.
Correct! Cyanosis = blue color from deoxygenated hemoglobin. 'Cyan' = blue-green. Check lips, nail beds, earlobes.
Yellowing is jaundice (icterus) from bilirubin accumulation - liver/bile problem, not respiratory.
Paleness (pallor) is from anemia or poor perfusion - not enough blood/hemoglobin, not deoxygenation.

mechanism.title: Blue Skin Oxygen Sign
mechanism.content: Cyanosis is bluish discoloration of skin and mucous membranes caused by elevated deoxygenated hemoglobin (>5 g/dL) in capillary blood. It appears first in areas with thin skin and high blood flow like lips, tongue, and nail beds. Central cyanosis indicates low arterial oxygen saturation, while peripheral cyanosis reflects sluggish local blood flow.
mechanism.metaphor: Like how a blue tint appears when you hold your breath underwater - deoxygenated blood looks bluish-purple through skin, signaling that hemoglobin is running around without its oxygen cargo.

questions.explain: Tachypnea is abnormally rapid breathing rate (>20 breaths/min in adults). Tachy- = fast, -pnea = breathing.

optionExplains.text: Slow breathing is bradypnea (brady- = slow). Opposite of tachypnea.
Correct! Tachypnea = fast breathing rate. Tachy- = rapid (like tachycardia = fast heart). Can be from fever, anxiety, lung disease.
Difficult breathing is dyspnea (dys- = difficult). Tachypnea is about RATE, not effort.
No breathing is apnea (a- = without). Tachypnea is breathing, just too fast.

mechanism.title: Rapid Breathing Rate
mechanism.content: Tachypnea is abnormally rapid breathing rate (>20 breaths/min in adults) that may or may not involve increased tidal volume. It compensates for hypoxia, hypercapnia, metabolic acidosis, or increased metabolic demand like fever or exercise. Unlike hyperventilation, tachypnea doesn't necessarily cause hypocapnia and respiratory alkalosis.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a dog panting on a hot day - quick, shallow breaths that move air rapidly without necessarily taking bigger gulps, often triggered by oxygen need or body heat rather than conscious control.

questions.explain: Bradypnea is abnormally slow breathing rate (<12 breaths/min in adults). Brady- = slow, -pnea = breathing.

optionExplains.text: Rapid breathing is tachypnea (tachy- = fast). Opposite of bradypnea.
Correct! Bradypnea = slow breathing. Brady- = slow (like bradycardia = slow heart). Can be from opioids, brain injury, sleep.
Deep breathing is hyperpnea (increased depth). Bradypnea is about RATE, not depth.
Shallow breathing might be called hypopnea. Bradypnea specifically means slow rate.

mechanism.title: Slow Breathing Rate
mechanism.content: Bradypnea is abnormally slow breathing rate (<12 breaths/min in adults) that can result from respiratory center depression by drugs, brain injury, or metabolic alkalosis. Slow breathing reduces minute ventilation, potentially causing hypercapnia and respiratory acidosis. It contrasts with normal sleep-related breathing rate reduction.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a clock running in slow motion - your breathing drops below normal pace, often from sedatives or brain injury suppressing the automatic breathing centers, risking CO2 buildup.

questions.explain: Orthopnea is dyspnea when lying down, relieved by sitting up. Ortho- = straight/upright, -pnea = breathing.

optionExplains.text: Correct! Orthopnea = must sit upright to breathe comfortably. Classic sign of heart failure - fluid redistributes when lying down.
Breathing during sleep would involve sleep apnea or normal respiration - not specifically orthopnea.
Normal breathing is eupnea (eu- = good/normal). Orthopnea is abnormal positional dyspnea.
Rapid breathing is tachypnea. Orthopnea is about POSITION (lying vs sitting), not rate.

mechanism.title: Upright-Only Breathing
mechanism.content: Orthopnea is dyspnea that occurs or worsens when lying flat and is relieved by sitting or standing upright. It results from increased venous return and pulmonary blood volume in recumbent position, worsening pulmonary congestion in heart failure. Patients often sleep propped on multiple pillows to maintain an upright position.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a flood that only happens in low-lying areas - when you lie down, extra blood pools in your lungs like water collecting in a valley, making breathing harder until you sit upright and drain the excess.

questions.explain: Hyperventilation = breathing beyond metabolic needs, blowing off excess CO2, causing respiratory alkalosis (high pH).

optionExplains.text: Breathing too slowly is hypoventilation - it RETAINS CO2 instead of blowing it off.
Correct! Hyperventilation = excessive ventilation. Blows off CO2 -> hypocapnia -> alkalosis. Causes: anxiety, fever, pain.
Holding breath is breath-holding or apnea - no air movement at all, not excessive movement.
Normal breathing is eupnea. Hyperventilation is pathologically EXCESSIVE ventilation.

mechanism.title: Excess Ventilation Blowoff
mechanism.content: Hyperventilation is breathing in excess of metabolic needs, lowering arterial CO2 (hypocapnia) and causing respiratory alkalosis. It can be triggered by anxiety, pain, hypoxia, or voluntary overbreathing. Low CO2 causes cerebral vasoconstriction (dizziness), increased neuromuscular excitability (tingling, tetany), and shifts the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve leftward.
mechanism.metaphor: Like over-ventilating a room until it's too cold - breathing too much blows off CO2 faster than cells produce it, making blood alkaline and triggering tingling sensations like your body's complaining about the imbalance.

questions.explain: Hypoventilation = breathing insufficient to meet metabolic needs, causing CO2 retention (hypercapnia) and respiratory acidosis.

optionExplains.text: Breathing too fast is hyperventilation (hyper- = excessive). Hypoventilation is the opposite - too LITTLE.
Correct! Hypoventilation = inadequate alveolar ventilation. CO2 builds up, pH drops. Causes: opioids, obesity, neuromuscular disease.
Deep breathing is hyperpnea - it can be normal (exercise) or pathological. Hypoventilation is about insufficient gas exchange.
Breath holding is apnea. Hypoventilation is slow/shallow breathing, not complete cessation.

mechanism.title: Shallow Breathing Crisis
mechanism.content: Hypoventilation occurs when alveolar ventilation is insufficient to remove CO2 produced by metabolism, causing respiratory acidosis. The reduced minute ventilation means CO2 accumulates faster than it can be exhaled, raising arterial PCO2 above 45 mmHg.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a fireplace with a blocked chimney - the smoke (CO2) keeps building up because the ventilation system can't clear it fast enough.

questions.explain: Boyle's law (P1V1 = P2V2): at constant temperature, pressure and volume are inversely related. Thoracic expansion lowers pressure, air flows in.

optionExplains.text: Gas solubility in liquids is Henry's law. Boyle's law is about pressure-volume, not dissolving.
Correct! Boyle's law: when volume increases, pressure decreases. Diaphragm contracts -> thorax expands -> pressure drops -> air rushes in.
Partial pressures of gases is Dalton's law. Boyle's law doesn't deal with gas mixtures.
Diffusion across membranes is Fick's law. Boyle's law is about bulk air movement, not molecular diffusion.

mechanism.title: Pressure-Volume Seesaw
mechanism.content: Boyle's Law states that at constant temperature, gas pressure and volume are inversely related (P1V1 = P2V2). During inspiration, thoracic volume increases which decreases pressure, causing air to flow into the lungs down its pressure gradient.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a bicycle pump in reverse - pull the plunger out (increase volume) and pressure inside drops, sucking air in.

options: How gas dissolves in liquid based on pressure
questions.explain: Henry's law: the amount of gas dissolved in liquid is proportional to its partial pressure. Higher PO2 = more dissolved O2.

optionExplains.text: Lung mechanics (pressure-volume) is Boyle's law. Henry's law is about gas solubility.
Correct! Henry's law explains why high-pressure diving causes nitrogen narcosis and decompression sickness - more gas dissolves at depth.
Airway resistance relates to Poiseuille's law (flow through tubes). Henry's law is about dissolution.
O2-hemoglobin binding follows the dissociation curve (Bohr effect). Henry's law is about gas dissolving in plasma, not binding.

mechanism.title: Pressure Drives Dissolution
mechanism.content: Henry's Law states that the amount of gas dissolving in liquid is directly proportional to its partial pressure above the liquid. Higher alveolar PO2 drives more oxygen into solution in blood plasma, where it can bind to hemoglobin.
mechanism.metaphor: Like carbonation in soda - higher CO2 pressure forces more gas to dissolve in the liquid; open the cap (drop pressure) and bubbles escape.

questions.q: Dalton's law of partial pressures states:
options: Total pressure equals sum of individual gas pressures
Pressure and volume are inversely related
Gas moves from high to low pressure
questions.explain: Dalton's law: in a gas mixture, total pressure = sum of partial pressures. Each gas acts independently regardless of other gases present.

optionExplains.text: Correct! P_total = P_O2 + P_CO2 + P_N2 + P_H2O. At sea level: 760 = 159 + 0.3 + 597 + 3.7 mmHg (approximately).
Pressure-volume inverse relationship is Boyle's law (P1V1 = P2V2), not Dalton's.
Gas moving down pressure gradients is Fick's law of diffusion. Dalton's law just describes partial pressures.
Solubility depending on pressure is Henry's law. Dalton's law is about gas mixtures, not dissolution.

mechanism.title: Independent Gas Pressures
mechanism.content: Dalton's Law states that total gas pressure equals the sum of partial pressures of individual gases (Ptotal = PO2 + PCO2 + PN2 + ...). Each gas exerts pressure independently proportional to its percentage in the mixture.
mechanism.metaphor: Like roommates sharing rent - each person contributes their portion independently, and all portions add up to the total bill.

questions.q: The partial pressure of oxygen in atmospheric air is approximately:
questions.explain: At sea level: 760 mmHg total x 21% O2 = ~159 mmHg. This is the starting point before air reaches alveoli.

optionExplains.text: 78 mmHg is approximately the venous PO2 (40-75 mmHg range). Atmospheric is much higher.
104 mmHg is alveolar PO2 (after humidification and CO2 mixing). Atmospheric is higher because it's 'fresh' air.
Correct! 760 mmHg x 0.21 = 159.6 mmHg. Remember: 21% O2 in air. At altitude, total pressure drops so PO2 drops too.
40 mmHg is the PO2 of venous blood or the PCO2 of arterial blood. Not atmospheric O2.

mechanism.title: Sea Level Oxygen
mechanism.content: At sea level with atmospheric pressure of 760 mmHg, oxygen comprises 21% of dry air, giving a partial pressure of approximately 159 mmHg (0.21 x 760). This is the driving pressure for oxygen diffusion into the respiratory system.
mechanism.metaphor: Like the water pressure at the top of a fountain - this is the starting pressure that drives the entire cascade downward into the lungs.

questions.q: The partial pressure of oxygen in alveolar air is approximately:
questions.explain: Alveolar PO2 (~104 mmHg) is lower than atmospheric (~159 mmHg) because air is humidified and mixed with CO2.

optionExplains.text: 40 mmHg is venous PO2 (blood returning to lungs) or arterial PCO2. Not alveolar O2.
Correct! Alveolar PO2 = 104 mmHg. Why lower than 159? Water vapor (47 mmHg) and CO2 (40 mmHg) dilute the O2.
159 mmHg is ATMOSPHERIC PO2. By the time air reaches alveoli, humidification and gas exchange lower it.
95 mmHg is close to arterial PO2 (95-100 mmHg). Alveolar is slightly higher to create diffusion gradient.

mechanism.title: Diluted By Humidity
mechanism.content: Alveolar PO2 drops to ~104 mmHg because inspired air is humidified (adding water vapor pressure of 47 mmHg) and mixes with residual alveolar gas containing CO2. The alveolar gas equation accounts for this dilution effect.
mechanism.metaphor: Like adding ice to a drink - the original concentration gets diluted by the added water (vapor) and leftover liquid (residual gas) from the last sip.

questions.q: The normal partial pressure of CO2 in arterial blood is:
questions.explain: Normal arterial PCO2 (PaCO2) is 35-45 mmHg, average 40 mmHg. It's regulated by ventilation rate.

optionExplains.text: 100 mmHg is approximately arterial PO2, not PCO2. CO2 is much lower because it's being exhaled.
Correct! PaCO2 = 40 mmHg normally. Higher = hypoventilation (acidosis). Lower = hyperventilation (alkalosis).
46 mmHg is approximately VENOUS PCO2 (45-46 mmHg) - slightly higher because tissues add CO2. Arterial is lower.
20 mmHg would be severe hypocapnia from extreme hyperventilation - not normal.

mechanism.title: CO2 Set Point
mechanism.content: Arterial PCO2 is tightly regulated at ~40 mmHg by chemoreceptors that adjust ventilation rate. This balance represents equilibrium between metabolic CO2 production (~200 mL/min) and alveolar elimination.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a thermostat maintaining room temperature - sensors constantly measure the level and adjust the ventilation fan to keep it at the set point.

questions.q: The oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve shifts RIGHT with:
questions.explain: The Bohr effect: increased CO2, decreased pH, increased temperature, and increased 2,3-DPG all shift the curve right, promoting O2 release to tissues.

optionExplains.text: DECREASED temperature shifts the curve LEFT (hemoglobin holds onto O2). Fever shifts it RIGHT.
INCREASED pH (alkalosis) shifts LEFT. DECREASED pH (acidosis) shifts RIGHT - active tissues are acidic and need O2.
Correct! More CO2 = lower pH = right shift = easier O2 release. This is the Bohr effect: delivers O2 where metabolism is high.
DECREASED 2,3-DPG shifts LEFT. Increased 2,3-DPG (from anemia, altitude) shifts RIGHT to compensate.

mechanism.title: Rightward Release Signal
mechanism.content: The oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve shifts right when tissues need more O2 (increased temperature, CO2, H+, or 2,3-BPG). This decreases hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen, promoting release to metabolically active tissues.
mechanism.metaphor: Like loosening your grip on a package when it gets hot - the cargo (O2) becomes easier to release exactly when the recipient (tissues) needs it most.

options: CO2 transport enhanced when O2 is unloaded
questions.explain: Haldane effect: deoxygenated hemoglobin binds more CO2 (as carbaminohemoglobin) than oxygenated hemoglobin. It's the 'mirror' of the Bohr effect.

optionExplains.text: O2 binding is described by the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve, not the Haldane effect.
Correct! Haldane effect: deoxy-Hb is better at carrying CO2. At tissues (low O2), Hb unloads O2 AND picks up CO2. At lungs (high O2), reverse happens.
Surfactant is produced by type II alveolar cells - unrelated to the Haldane effect.
Airway resistance relates to Poiseuille's law and bronchial diameter - not CO2 transport.

mechanism.title: Deoxygenation Helps Loading
mechanism.content: The Haldane effect describes how deoxygenated hemoglobin binds CO2 and H+ more readily than oxygenated hemoglobin. In tissues, as O2 unloads, Hb's increased affinity for CO2 facilitates waste removal; in lungs, O2 loading promotes CO2 release.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a two-door elevator - as passengers (O2) exit one door in tissues, it creates room for waste (CO2) to enter the other door; reverse happens in lungs.

questions.explain: Chloride shift (Hamburger phenomenon): as HCO3- exits RBCs to plasma, Cl- enters to maintain electrical neutrality across the membrane.

optionExplains.text: Sodium exchange isn't the chloride shift - sodium is cation, this involves anion exchange (Cl- for HCO3-).
Correct! In tissues: CO2 enters RBC -> becomes HCO3- -> exits to plasma -> Cl- enters. Reversed at lungs. Maintains RBC charge balance.
Potassium transport isn't the chloride shift. K+ is a cation; this shift involves anions.
Calcium signaling isn't involved in CO2 transport or the chloride shift.

mechanism.title: Chloride-Bicarbonate Swap
mechanism.content: As CO2 enters RBCs and converts to bicarbonate (HCO3-), the bicarbonate exits to plasma while chloride (Cl-) enters to maintain electrical neutrality. This chloride shift enables buffering of CO2 without changing RBC charge balance.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a store exchange policy - as bicarbonate leaves the cell (returns product), chloride enters (replacement item) to keep the charge balance sheet even.

questions.explain: Carbaminohemoglobin: ~23% of CO2 binds directly to hemoglobin's amino groups (not heme). This is distinct from bicarbonate transport.

optionExplains.text: Hemoglobin bound to O2 is oxyhemoglobin (HbO2). The 'oxy' prefix indicates oxygen.
Correct! Carbamino-hemoglobin: CO2 binds to amino groups on the protein (not heme). It's ~23% of CO2 transport.
Hemoglobin bound to CO is carboxyhemoglobin (HbCO) - DEADLY because CO binds 200x stronger than O2.
Oxidized hemoglobin (methemoglobin) has Fe3+ instead of Fe2+ - it can't carry O2. Caused by nitrites, drugs.

mechanism.title: Direct CO2 Binding
mechanism.content: About 5-10% of CO2 binds directly to amino groups on hemoglobin forming carbaminohemoglobin, without requiring conversion to bicarbonate. Deoxygenated hemoglobin binds CO2 more readily than oxygenated form (Haldane effect).
mechanism.metaphor: Like a taxi with a bike rack - some cargo (CO2) attaches directly to the outside of the vehicle (hemoglobin) rather than going through the trunk (bicarbonate conversion).

questions.explain: Carbonic anhydrase catalyzes: CO2 + H2O <-> H2CO3 <-> H+ + HCO3-. It's one of the fastest enzymes known.

optionExplains.text: Surfactant is broken down by macrophages in alveoli - carbonic anhydrase isn't involved.
Correct! Carbonic anhydrase speeds up CO2 hydration 5000x. Without it, CO2 conversion would be too slow for gas exchange.
Oxygen transport is via hemoglobin, not an enzyme. Carbonic anhydrase deals with CO2.
Mucus is produced by goblet cells - secretion, not enzyme catalysis.

mechanism.title: CO2 Conversion Catalyst
mechanism.content: Carbonic anhydrase in RBCs catalyzes the reversible reaction CO2 + H2O - H2CO3 - H+ + HCO3-, accelerating it ~5000-fold. This enzyme is essential for efficient CO2 transport as bicarbonate (~70% of total CO2).
mechanism.metaphor: Like an assembly line supervisor - speeds up the conversion process thousands of times faster than workers (molecules) could manage on their own.

options: Air in conducting airways that doesn't exchange gas
questions.explain: Anatomical dead space (~150 mL) = air in trachea, bronchi, bronchioles - conducting airways with no gas exchange.

optionExplains.text: Air in alveoli IS where gas exchange occurs - it's the opposite of dead space.
Correct! Anatomical dead space = conducting zone air. Of 500 mL tidal volume, only ~350 mL reaches alveoli.
Residual volume is air that CAN'T be exhaled (~1200 mL). Dead space is about WHERE air is, not whether it can be exhaled.
Expiratory reserve is extra air you CAN exhale beyond tidal volume. Different concept from dead space.

mechanism.title: Conducting Zone Volume
mechanism.content: Anatomical dead space (~150 mL) is the volume of airways that conduct air but don't participate in gas exchange (nose to terminal bronchioles). This means the first 150 mL of each breath is wasted air from the last exhalation.
mechanism.metaphor: Like the pipes leading to a shower - water sits in the plumbing doing nothing useful until it reaches the showerhead (alveoli) where it actually works.

questions.explain: FRC = ERV + RV = TLC - VC. It's the air remaining after normal, relaxed expiration (~2400 mL).

optionExplains.text: TV + IRV = Inspiratory Capacity (IC), not FRC. This is air you CAN inhale from rest.
Correct formula! RV + ERV = FRC. But C is also correct, so 'both B and C' is the best answer.
Also correct! TLC - VC = FRC. Since VC = IRV + TV + ERV, subtracting leaves RV + ERV = FRC.
Correct! Both formulas work: FRC = RV + ERV = TLC - VC. Two ways to calculate the same thing.

mechanism.title: Resting Air Reserve
mechanism.content: FRC (~2400 mL) is the volume remaining in lungs at the end of passive exhalation, where inward elastic recoil equals outward chest wall tension. This reservoir prevents alveolar collapse and provides oxygen between breaths.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a emergency generator battery - maintains a baseline charge (gas volume) between power cycles (breaths) so the system never fully shuts down.

questions.q: Total lung capacity in adults is approximately:
questions.explain: TLC = ~6000 mL (6 L) in average adult male. It's the maximum air the lungs can hold after full inspiration.

optionExplains.text: 2000 mL is close to FRC (functional residual capacity). TLC is much larger.
4000 mL is approximately vital capacity (VC). TLC includes residual volume too.
Correct! TLC = ~6 L = VC (~4.8 L) + RV (~1.2 L). Varies with height, sex, age.
8000 mL would be exceptionally large - elite athletes might approach this, but 6 L is average.

mechanism.title: Maximum Lung Volume
mechanism.content: TLC (~6000 mL in adult males) is the volume in lungs after maximum inspiration, limited by chest wall and respiratory muscle strength. It represents the sum of all lung volumes: vital capacity plus residual volume.
mechanism.metaphor: Like filling a balloon to its absolute maximum - you can't add more air because the elastic limits of the container (thorax and lungs) have been reached.

questions.q: The term 'compliance' in respiratory physiology refers to:
questions.explain: Compliance = ΔV/ΔP (change in volume per unit pressure change). High compliance = easy to expand; low compliance = stiff.

optionExplains.text: Airway resistance is about flow obstruction (bronchoconstriction, mucus). Compliance is about tissue stretch.
Correct! Compliance = how easily lungs expand. Think of a balloon: easy to inflate = high compliance; hard = low compliance.
Gas exchange efficiency relates to diffusion capacity and V/Q matching - not the same as compliance.
Breathing rate is respiratory frequency. Compliance affects the WORK of breathing, not the rate.

mechanism.title: Stretchability Coefficient
mechanism.content: Compliance (dV/dP) measures how easily lungs expand per unit pressure change, typically ~200 mL/cm H2O. High compliance means lungs inflate easily; low compliance requires more work to breathe.
mechanism.metaphor: Like comparing a balloon to a tire - the balloon (high compliance) expands easily with a little puff, while the tire (low compliance) needs a pump.

questions.explain: Emphysema destroys alveolar walls and elastic tissue, making lungs TOO compliant - easy to inflate but hard to deflate (air trapping).

optionExplains.text: DECREASED compliance is fibrosis (stiff lungs). Emphysema is the opposite problem.
Correct! Emphysema = INCREASED compliance (floppy lungs). Lost elasticity means air gets trapped. Barrel chest appearance.
Emphysema definitely changes compliance - it's a defining feature of the disease.
Compliance change is consistent in emphysema - always increased (loss of recoil).

mechanism.title: Floppy Lungs Paradox
mechanism.content: Emphysema destroys alveolar walls and elastic fibers, increasing compliance - lungs inflate too easily but don't recoil well during expiration. Patients have hyperinflation, air trapping, and must actively force air out.
mechanism.metaphor: Like an overstretched sweater - it's easy to pull on (inflate) but won't spring back to shape (deflate) because the elastic fibers are destroyed.

questions.q: Pulmonary fibrosis causes lung compliance to:
questions.explain: Fibrosis = scarring. Stiff, fibrous tissue replaces normal lung, making it hard to expand (DECREASED compliance).

optionExplains.text: INCREASED compliance is emphysema (floppy lungs). Fibrosis is the opposite - stiff lungs.
Correct! Fibrosis = DECREASED compliance (stiff lungs). More pressure needed to inflate. Restrictive lung disease.
Fibrosis significantly affects compliance - it's a restrictive disease because expansion is limited.
Compliance change is consistent - always decreased in fibrosis (scarring makes tissue stiff).

mechanism.title: Stiff Lung Syndrome
mechanism.content: Pulmonary fibrosis deposits collagen in lung tissue, decreasing compliance - lungs become stiff and require greater pressure to inflate. Patients have restrictive disease with reduced lung volumes and rapid, shallow breathing.
mechanism.metaphor: Like trying to inflate a leather bag instead of a paper bag - the stiff material resists expansion, requiring much more effort to breathe.

questions.explain: Phrenic nerve (C3-C5) is essential for diaphragm function. Mnemonic: 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive.'

optionExplains.text: Intercostal muscles are innervated by intercostal nerves (T1-T11), not the phrenic nerve.
Correct! Phrenic nerve = sole motor supply to diaphragm. C3-C5 origin. Damage = paralyzed hemidiaphragm.
Larynx is innervated by vagus nerve branches (recurrent laryngeal, superior laryngeal).
Pharynx is innervated by vagus and glossopharyngeal nerves, not phrenic.

mechanism.title: Diaphragm Power Cable
mechanism.content: The phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5) innervates the diaphragm, the primary muscle of respiration. Spinal injury above C3 causes respiratory failure because the diaphragm can't contract; 'C3-4-5 keeps the diaphragm alive.'
mechanism.metaphor: Like the power cord to your refrigerator - if the connection is severed above the plug (C3), the whole appliance (breathing) shuts down.

questions.q: The external intercostal muscles primarily assist with:
questions.explain: External intercostals elevate the ribs during inspiration, expanding the thorax like a bucket handle.

optionExplains.text: Expiration uses INTERNAL intercostals (and is mostly passive during quiet breathing).
Correct! EXTERNAL intercostals = inspiration. They run downward-forward and lift ribs up and out. 'E for Elevate, E for External.'
Coughing uses abdominal muscles primarily, plus internal intercostals for forced expiration.
Speaking uses laryngeal muscles - intercostals just provide the air pressure.

mechanism.title: Ribcage Elevator Muscles
mechanism.content: External intercostals run downward and forward between ribs; when they contract, they lift the ribcage up and out, increasing thoracic volume for inspiration. They work synergistically with the diaphragm during normal breathing.
mechanism.metaphor: Like bucket handles lifting up and out - pulling the handles (ribs) upward increases the bucket's (thorax's) capacity.

questions.q: The internal intercostal muscles primarily assist with:
questions.explain: Internal intercostals depress the ribs during forced expiration - they're not needed for quiet breathing.

optionExplains.text: Quiet breathing is mostly passive (elastic recoil) - internal intercostals aren't active.
Correct! INTERNAL intercostals = forced expiration. They run downward-backward and pull ribs down and in. Used in coughing, exercise.
Sneezing uses internal intercostals PLUS abdominal muscles - not 'only' sneezing.
Inspiration uses EXTERNAL intercostals (external = elevate = inhale).

mechanism.title: Active Exhalation Muscles
mechanism.content: Internal intercostals run upward and forward; they pull ribs downward and inward, decreasing thoracic volume during forced expiration (exercise, coughing). Normal quiet breathing doesn't require their active contraction.
mechanism.metaphor: Like squeezing a accordion closed - these muscles actively compress the bellows (ribcage) to force air out faster than passive recoil alone.

options: Thin squamous cells for gas exchange
questions.explain: Type I pneumocytes cover 95% of alveolar surface area - extremely thin (0.1-0.2 µm) for rapid gas diffusion.

optionExplains.text: Surfactant-producing cells are Type II pneumocytes, not Type I.
Correct! Type I cells = simple squamous epithelium. Cover 95% of surface. So thin that gas diffuses through easily.
Immune cells in alveoli are macrophages (dust cells), not Type I pneumocytes.
Mucus-producing cells are goblet cells in conducting airways, not alveoli.

mechanism.title: Gas Exchange Pavement
mechanism.content: Type I pneumocytes cover ~95% of alveolar surface area with extremely thin squamous cells (~0.2 um thick), optimized for rapid gas diffusion. Their large surface area and minimal thickness minimize diffusion distance.
mechanism.metaphor: Like saran wrap stretched over a bowl - incredibly thin coverage (cells) that lets gas pass through easily while keeping structure intact.

questions.explain: Type II pneumocytes are cuboidal cells that secrete surfactant and can divide to replace damaged Type I cells.

optionExplains.text: Mucus is produced by goblet cells in airways, not Type II alveolar cells.
Correct! Type II cells = surfactant factories. They're cuboidal, only 5% of surface area but critical for preventing collapse.
Hemoglobin is made by red blood cell precursors in bone marrow, not lung cells.
Carbonic anhydrase is in RBCs (and some other tissues), not specifically Type II cells.

mechanism.title: Surfactant Factories
mechanism.content: Type II pneumocytes (~5% of surface area) are cuboidal cells that secrete pulmonary surfactant, which reduces surface tension to prevent alveolar collapse. They also serve as progenitor cells that can differentiate into Type I cells.
mechanism.metaphor: Like soap dispensers in a bubble-blowing kit - they produce the special liquid (surfactant) that keeps bubbles (alveoli) from popping.

questions.explain: Dust cells are phagocytic macrophages that patrol alveoli, engulfing bacteria, particles, and debris.

optionExplains.text: Goblet cells produce mucus in airways - they're secretory, not phagocytic.
Correct! Dust cells = alveolar macrophages. They 'eat' inhaled particles and pathogens. Named for cleaning up 'dust.'
Clara cells (now called Club cells) are in bronchioles - they secrete protective proteins, not phagocytose.
Mast cells release histamine in allergic reactions - they're not macrophages.

mechanism.title: Lung Cleanup Crew
mechanism.content: Alveolar macrophages patrol the alveolar surface, phagocytosing inhaled particles, bacteria, and debris. They migrate up the mucociliary escalator or into lymphatics, keeping the gas exchange surface clean.
mechanism.metaphor: Like Roomba vacuum robots - constantly patrolling the floor (alveolar surface) to clean up dust and dirt particles before they cause problems.

questions.q: The mucociliary escalator moves debris toward the:
questions.explain: Cilia beat in coordinated waves, propelling mucus (with trapped particles) upward toward the throat to be swallowed or expectorated.

optionExplains.text: Moving debris TOWARD alveoli would be disastrous - the escalator moves it AWAY from alveoli.
Correct! Mucus escalator moves UP toward pharynx (throat). Then swallowed or coughed out. 'Escalator' = going up!
Bronchioles are deeper in the lung - the escalator moves debris OUT, not deeper in.
Pleura is the lung membrane - debris doesn't go there. It goes up and out via the airway.

mechanism.title: Mucus Conveyor Belt
mechanism.content: Cilia on respiratory epithelium beat in coordinated waves (~12 Hz) to propel a mucus layer upward toward the pharynx at ~1-2 cm/minute. This escalator traps and removes inhaled particles before they reach alveoli.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a airport moving walkway with sticky floor - the constantly moving belt (beating cilia) carries luggage (trapped particles) up and out of the terminal.

questions.explain: Asthma = REVERSIBLE obstructive airway disease. Triggers cause bronchospasm, mucus production, and airway edema.

optionExplains.text: Alveolar destruction is emphysema, not asthma. Asthma affects airways, not alveoli.
Correct! Asthma = inflamed, hyperreactive airways that constrict. Key word: REVERSIBLE (bronchodilators help). Triggers: allergens, exercise, cold air.
Pleural effusion is fluid around the lung - unrelated to asthma's airway inflammation.
Lung cancer is malignancy, not the inflammatory/bronchospastic process of asthma.

mechanism.title: Reversible Airway Squeeze
mechanism.content: Asthma causes reversible bronchospasm, inflammation, and mucus hypersecretion triggered by allergens, exercise, or irritants. Smooth muscle contraction narrows airways (wheezing), but responds to bronchodilators unlike fixed obstructions.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a garden hose someone is squeezing - the blockage (bronchospasm) can be relieved by letting go (bronchodilators), unlike a kink (structural damage).

questions.q: COPD includes which two main conditions?
questions.explain: COPD = Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease. Two main types: emphysema (alveolar destruction) and chronic bronchitis (airway inflammation/mucus).

optionExplains.text: Asthma is REVERSIBLE obstruction - COPD is largely IRREVERSIBLE. Different disease category.
Correct! COPD = emphysema ('pink puffer' - barrel chest) + chronic bronchitis ('blue bloater' - cough/sputum). Often coexist.
Pneumonia and pleurisy are acute infections/inflammation, not chronic obstructive diseases.
TB and lung cancer are serious but aren't the components of COPD.

mechanism.title: Permanent Airflow Block
mechanism.content: COPD combines emphysema (alveolar destruction, loss of elastic recoil) with chronic bronchitis (airway inflammation, mucus hypersecretion). This creates irreversible airflow limitation, unlike asthma which is reversible.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a collapsed and clogged storm drain - both the structure is damaged (emphysema) and debris blocks flow (chronic bronchitis), making obstruction permanent.

questions.q: In emphysema, the primary pathology is:
questions.explain: Emphysema = destruction of alveolar septa by protease enzymes (especially from smoking). Reduces gas exchange surface area.

optionExplains.text: Airway inflammation is more characteristic of chronic bronchitis and asthma, not emphysema.
Correct! Emphysema = alveolar wall destruction. Creates large air spaces ('bullae'). Lost elastic recoil causes air trapping.
Excess mucus production is chronic bronchitis ('blue bloater'), not emphysema.
Pleural thickening isn't a feature of emphysema - it might occur with asbestosis or other diseases.

mechanism.title: Alveolar Wall Demolition
mechanism.content: Emphysema results from destruction of alveolar walls and elastic fibers, usually by smoking-induced protease-antiprotease imbalance. Loss of alveoli reduces surface area for gas exchange and eliminates elastic recoil needed for passive expiration.
mechanism.metaphor: Like removing walls between rooms in a house - you get a bigger open space (air trapping) but lose structural support and functional compartments (gas exchange surface).

options: Productive cough for 3 months in 2 consecutive years
questions.explain: Clinical definition: productive cough (with sputum) for at least 3 months per year for 2 consecutive years. Usually smoking-related.

optionExplains.text: ACUTE infection is acute bronchitis - different from CHRONIC bronchitis which is defined by duration.
Correct! The '3 months, 2 years' rule is the clinical definition. Distinguishes chronic bronchitis from acute or occasional cough.
Collapsed lung (pneumothorax/atelectasis) is unrelated to chronic bronchitis.
Pulmonary embolism is a blood clot in lung vessels - different pathology entirely.

mechanism.title: Three Month Cough Rule
mechanism.content: Chronic bronchitis is clinically defined as productive cough for at least 3 months per year for 2 consecutive years, without other cause. Smoking triggers goblet cell hyperplasia and chronic airway inflammation.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a factory with overactive production lines - the mucus-making cells (goblet cells) work overtime for months, clogging the facility with excess product.

questions.explain: Pulmonary edema = fluid in alveoli/interstitium, impairing gas exchange. Most commonly from left heart failure (cardiogenic).

optionExplains.text: Air in pleural space is pneumothorax, not pulmonary edema.
Correct! Pulmonary edema = fluid flooding alveoli. Causes: heart failure (backup into lungs), ARDS, drowning. 'Wet lungs.'
Collapsed airways might be bronchoconstriction or tumor compression - not edema.
Lung cancer is malignancy - different pathology from fluid accumulation.

mechanism.title: Flooded Alveoli
mechanism.content: Pulmonary edema occurs when fluid leaks from capillaries into alveolar spaces due to increased hydrostatic pressure (heart failure) or increased permeability (ARDS, toxins). Fluid impairs gas diffusion causing hypoxemia.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a basement flooding - water (fluid) fills the living space (alveoli) where it doesn't belong, making the room unusable for its intended purpose (gas exchange).

questions.q: Pulmonary embolism is typically caused by:
questions.explain: PE = blood clot lodging in pulmonary artery. Usually from DVT (deep vein thrombosis) in legs - the clot breaks off and travels to lungs.

optionExplains.text: Bacterial infection causes pneumonia or septic emboli, not typical PE.
Correct! PE usually = DVT clot that traveled to lungs. Risk factors: immobility, surgery, cancer, clotting disorders, flights. Can be fatal.
Allergic reaction causes anaphylaxis or asthma - not embolism.
Inhalation injury affects airways directly - not the same as a blood clot blocking pulmonary vessels.

mechanism.title: Circulation Roadblock
mechanism.content: Pulmonary embolism occurs when a blood clot (usually from deep leg veins) lodges in pulmonary arteries, blocking blood flow. This creates ventilation-perfusion mismatch - ventilated alveoli receive no blood for gas exchange.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a truck jackknifed on a highway - traffic (blood) backs up behind the obstruction while downstream lanes (alveoli) sit empty despite being open.

questions.explain: Pneumonia = lung parenchyma infection (bacterial, viral, fungal). Alveoli fill with inflammatory exudate, causing consolidation.

optionExplains.text: Air in pleural space is pneumothorax (pneumo- = air, but different location).
Correct! Pneumonia = lung infection. Pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi) cause inflammation, pus, and fluid in alveoli.
Lung collapse is atelectasis (or pneumothorax). Pneumonia is infection, not collapse.
Cancer is malignancy. Pneumonia is infectious/inflammatory - different process.

mechanism.title: Alveolar Infection Battle
mechanism.content: Pneumonia is infection of lung parenchyma causing alveoli to fill with inflammatory exudate (neutrophils, fibrin, fluid). Consolidation impairs gas exchange and appears as opacification on chest X-ray.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a sponge soaked with glue - the normally air-filled pores (alveoli) become packed with sticky debris (exudate), losing their ability to function.

questions.explain: TB (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) prefers lung apices - higher oxygen tension favors the aerobic bacteria. Look for upper lobe cavities on CXR.

optionExplains.text: Bronchi can be involved but TB classically affects lung parenchyma, especially apices.
Correct! TB loves the lung APICES (upper lobes). Higher O2, less blood flow, less immune surveillance. Ghon focus, cavitary lesions.
Laryngeal TB exists but is much less common than pulmonary TB.
Tracheal TB is rare. The lung parenchyma (especially apices) is the classic location.

mechanism.title: Apical Oxygen Preference
