mechanism.content: Mycobacterium tuberculosis preferentially infects lung apices because they have higher oxygen tension (PO2) due to better ventilation-perfusion ratio. The obligate aerobe thrives in this oxygen-rich environment.
mechanism.metaphor: Like mold growing on the top shelf - the organism prefers the well-ventilated upper area where oxygen (its preferred nutrient) is most abundant.

questions.explain: Bronchiectasis = irreversible bronchial widening from chronic infection/inflammation. '-ectasis' = dilation/expansion.

optionExplains.text: Bronchial constriction is bronchoconstriction (asthma). '-ectasis' means dilation, not constriction.
Correct! Bronchi-ectasis = dilated bronchi. Walls are damaged by infection. Causes: CF, chronic infections, immune deficiency. Lots of sputum.
Bronchial inflammation alone is bronchitis. Bronchiectasis is structural dilation from repeated damage.
Bronchial cancer would be bronchogenic carcinoma - not '-ectasis.'

mechanism.title: Bronchial Ballooning Disease
mechanism.content: Bronchiectasis is irreversible bronchial dilation from chronic infection or obstruction that destroys airway walls. Widened airways trap mucus, creating a vicious cycle of infection, inflammation, and further damage.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a balloon stretched too many times - the airways lose their elastic structure and stay permanently widened, becoming pooling sites for fluid (mucus) and bacteria.

questions.q: Cystic fibrosis affects the respiratory system by causing:
questions.explain: CF = defective CFTR chloride channel. Without Cl- secretion, mucus becomes thick and sticky, blocking airways and promoting infection.

optionExplains.text: Thin watery mucus would be the OPPOSITE of CF. CF mucus is pathologically THICK.
Correct! CF = thick, sticky mucus. CFTR mutation prevents Cl-/water secretion. Mucus plugs airways, bacteria thrive, lung damage ensues.
Surfactant deficiency is NRDS (neonatal respiratory distress syndrome), not CF.
Pleural scarring isn't the primary CF lung problem - it's the thick airway mucus.

mechanism.title: Thick Mucus Genetics
mechanism.content: Cystic fibrosis results from CFTR gene mutations causing defective chloride channels. Decreased chloride and water secretion produces abnormally thick, sticky mucus that obstructs airways, traps bacteria, and causes chronic lung infections.
mechanism.metaphor: Like replacing WD-40 with peanut butter in a lock - the thickened lubricant (mucus) clogs the mechanism (airways) instead of keeping it clean and moving smoothly.

questions.explain: Sleep apnea = recurrent episodes of breathing cessation (>10 sec) during sleep. Two types: obstructive (airway collapse) and central (brain signal failure).

optionExplains.text: Continuous breathing is normal. Apnea = pauses/stops in breathing.
Correct! Sleep apnea = breathing stops repeatedly during sleep. Causes snoring, daytime sleepiness, cardiovascular strain. Often treated with CPAP.
Increased breathing rate would be tachypnea. Apnea is the opposite - breathing STOPS.
Coughing at night might be asthma, GERD, or postnasal drip - not specifically sleep apnea.

mechanism.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
mechanism.content: During sleep, throat muscles relax excessively, causing the airway to collapse intermittently. Each blockage triggers oxygen desaturation until the brain briefly arouses to restore breathing, fragmenting sleep architecture.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a garden hose that gets kinked when you lay it down - water stops flowing until you shake it straight again, but it keeps happening all night.

questions.explain: Hering-Breuer = inflation reflex. Pulmonary stretch receptors sense lung expansion and inhibit further inspiration, preventing overinflation.

optionExplains.text: Lung collapse prevention involves surfactant and negative pleural pressure - not Hering-Breuer.
Correct! Hering-Breuer = 'stop signal' for inspiration. Stretch receptors in bronchi sense full lungs and tell brainstem to stop inhaling.
Coughing is a separate reflex triggered by airway irritants.
Sneezing is triggered by nasal irritation - different reflex entirely.

mechanism.title: Stretch-Activated Safety Brake
mechanism.content: Pulmonary stretch receptors detect when lungs reach near-maximal inflation and send inhibitory signals via the vagus nerve to the medullary respiratory center. This negative feedback prevents overinflation damage by terminating inspiration early.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a pressure relief valve on a tire pump - when the tire is full enough, the valve automatically stops more air from being forced in.

questions.explain: Central chemoreceptors in medulla detect H+ (from CO2) in CSF. CO2 crosses blood-brain barrier, becomes H+, and stimulates breathing.

optionExplains.text: Oxygen levels are mainly detected by PERIPHERAL chemoreceptors (carotid/aortic bodies).
Correct! Central chemoreceptors sense CSF pH (which reflects blood CO2). The main driver of normal breathing. 'Central' = brainstem.
Blood pressure is sensed by baroreceptors in carotid sinus/aortic arch - different system.
Lung stretch is sensed by pulmonary stretch receptors (Hering-Breuer) - not chemoreceptors.

mechanism.title: Brainstem pH Sensors
mechanism.content: Specialized neurons in the medulla monitor hydrogen ion concentration in cerebrospinal fluid. Rising CO2 diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, forms carbonic acid, lowers CSF pH, and triggers increased ventilation to restore normal levels.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a smoke detector in the basement of your house - it senses the earliest warning signs (acid buildup) before the problem spreads upstairs.

questions.q: Peripheral chemoreceptors are located in the:
questions.explain: Peripheral chemoreceptors in carotid bodies (CN IX) and aortic bodies (CN X) detect arterial O2, CO2, and pH.

optionExplains.text: Lungs have stretch receptors and irritant receptors, but chemoreceptors are in the vessels.
Correct! Carotid bodies (at carotid bifurcation) and aortic bodies (aortic arch) sense arterial blood gases. Especially important for O2 sensing.
Brain has CENTRAL chemoreceptors (medulla). PERIPHERAL chemoreceptors are outside the CNS.
Heart has stretch receptors but not the classical chemoreceptors for respiratory control.

mechanism.title: Arterial Oxygen Sentries
mechanism.content: Chemoreceptor cells in the carotid and aortic bodies continuously sample arterial blood for oxygen, CO2, and pH. When oxygen drops below 60 mmHg, they fire rapidly along cranial nerves IX and X to stimulate breathing.
mechanism.metaphor: Like border guards stationed at critical checkpoints - they constantly monitor what's passing through and sound the alarm when oxygen cargo runs dangerously low.

questions.q: The primary stimulus for breathing is:
questions.explain: CO2 (via H+) is the PRIMARY respiratory drive. Low O2 only becomes a major stimulus when very low (<60 mmHg).

optionExplains.text: Low oxygen DOES stimulate breathing, but only when severely low. CO2 is the main, moment-to-moment controller.
Correct! High CO2 -> low pH -> stimulates central/peripheral chemoreceptors -> increased ventilation. This is the normal minute-to-minute control.
Lung stretch inhibits breathing (Hering-Breuer) - it's a brake, not the main stimulus.
Voluntary control can override but isn't the PRIMARY autonomic stimulus.

mechanism.title: CO2 Drives Breathing
mechanism.content: Under normal conditions, rising arterial CO2 (not low oxygen) is the primary driver of ventilation. CO2 forms carbonic acid, lowering blood pH, which central and peripheral chemoreceptors detect to increase respiratory drive.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a thermostat that responds to heat buildup rather than cold - your body cares more about getting rid of CO2 waste than about oxygen shortage (until oxygen gets critically low).

options: Alternating periods of apnea and hyperpnea
questions.explain: Cheyne-Stokes = crescendo-decrescendo breathing with apneic pauses. Waxing and waning pattern. Often seen in heart failure, stroke, high altitude.

optionExplains.text: Steady breathing is normal (eupnea). Cheyne-Stokes is distinctly CYCLIC.
Correct! Cheyne-Stokes = breathing gradually deepens, then slows, then STOPS (apnea), then cycles again. Indicates brain/heart dysfunction.
Only rapid breathing would be tachypnea. Cheyne-Stokes has both fast and slow phases, plus pauses.
Only slow breathing is bradypnea. Cheyne-Stokes cycles through multiple rates.

mechanism.title: Waxing-Waning Breathing Cycles
mechanism.content: Cheyne-Stokes breathing features rhythmic crescendo-decrescendo tidal volumes separated by apneic pauses. It occurs when circulation delays between lungs and chemoreceptors cause oscillating feedback, common in heart failure or high altitude.
mechanism.metaphor: Like adjusting shower temperature with a long delay between the knob and the water - you overshoot hot, then cold, creating cycles because you can't feel the change immediately.

options: Deep rapid breathing seen in metabolic acidosis
questions.explain: Kussmaul = deep, rapid, labored breathing. The body's attempt to blow off CO2 and compensate for metabolic acidosis (e.g., diabetic ketoacidosis).

optionExplains.text: Shallow rapid breathing is different - Kussmaul is notably DEEP (large tidal volumes).
Correct! Kussmaul = deep AND rapid. Compensation for metabolic acidosis. Classic in DKA (diabetic ketoacidosis). 'Air hunger.'
Slow shallow breathing is hypoventilation - opposite of Kussmaul.
Breath holding is apnea. Kussmaul is continuous deep-fast breathing.

mechanism.title: Deep Acidosis Breathing
mechanism.content: Severe metabolic acidosis triggers deep, rapid, labored breathing as the body attempts respiratory compensation. By hyperventilating, CO2 is blown off to shift blood pH toward normal despite excess metabolic acids.
mechanism.metaphor: Like opening all windows and running fans on high to clear smoke from a house - the lungs work overtime to exhaust acid (CO2) when the body is overwhelmed by metabolic waste.

questions.explain: Biot's breathing = irregular, unpredictable breathing with random apneic pauses. Indicates severe brainstem damage or increased ICP.

optionExplains.text: Regular breathing is normal (eupnea). Biot's is chaotically IRREGULAR.
Correct! Biot's = random, unpredictable pattern with sudden pauses. Unlike Cheyne-Stokes (gradual), Biot's is chaotic. Bad prognosis.
Gradually increasing depth is part of Cheyne-Stokes (crescendo). Biot's doesn't have this gradual pattern.
Gradually decreasing rate is also Cheyne-Stokes territory. Biot's is randomly irregular.

mechanism.title: Chaotic Breathing Pattern
mechanism.content: Biot's breathing shows irregular depth and rhythm with unpredictable apneic periods, indicating severe brainstem damage affecting the medullary respiratory center. The normal coordinated breathing pattern disintegrates into random, ineffective cycles.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a broken metronome that ticks erratically - sometimes fast, sometimes slow, sometimes stopping - because the internal timing mechanism is damaged beyond reliable function.

questions.explain: Crackles (rales) = discontinuous, popping sounds. Caused by air bubbling through fluid or reopening of collapsed airways. Heard in pneumonia, pulmonary edema.

optionExplains.text: Normal breath sounds are vesicular (soft, breezy). Crackles are ABNORMAL adventitious sounds.
Correct! Crackles = fluid in airways/alveoli. Sound like Velcro or Rice Krispies. Fine crackles = alveolar; coarse crackles = larger airways.
Airway obstruction typically causes wheezing (continuous) not crackles (discontinuous).
Pleural friction is a friction RUB - grating, not popping. Different sound.

mechanism.title: Popping Airway Sounds
mechanism.content: Crackles (rales) are discontinuous popping sounds heard when collapsed small airways or alveoli suddenly snap open during inspiration. Fluid, pus, or exudate in the airways creates surface tension that must be overcome, producing audible pops.
mechanism.metaphor: Like pulling apart Velcro or stepping on bubble wrap - the sticky surfaces resist separating until enough pressure builds, then they suddenly release with a popping sound.

questions.explain: Wheezing = continuous, musical, whistling sound from turbulent airflow through narrowed airways. Classic in asthma, COPD, foreign body.

optionExplains.text: Fluid in alveoli causes crackles (discontinuous pops), not wheezing (continuous whistle).
Correct! Wheezing = narrow airways. Air squeaks through constriction. High-pitched = smaller airways. Polyphonic = multiple airways.
Pleural friction creates a friction RUB (grating), not wheezing.
Normal breathing has no wheezing. Wheezes are abnormal adventitious sounds.

mechanism.title: High-Pitched Airway Whistling
mechanism.content: Wheezing occurs when airflow accelerates through narrowed bronchioles, causing airway walls to vibrate at high frequency. Bronchoconstriction, mucus, or edema reduces airway diameter, creating the musical whistling sound typically on expiration.
mechanism.metaphor: Like blowing air through pursed lips or a narrow gap in a balloon - the smaller the opening, the higher pitched the whistle as air rushes through.

questions.q: Stridor is a sound typically caused by:
questions.explain: Stridor = high-pitched, harsh sound from turbulent airflow through narrowed UPPER airway (larynx/trachea). Medical emergency in children.

optionExplains.text: Alveolar fluid causes crackles. Stridor is from UPPER airways, not alveoli.
Correct! Stridor = upper airway (supraglottic, glottic, subglottic) obstruction. Inspiratory stridor is classic. Causes: croup, epiglottitis, foreign body.
Pleural effusion causes decreased breath sounds, not stridor.
Normal breathing doesn't produce stridor. It's an ABNORMAL sound indicating obstruction.

mechanism.title: Upper Airway Turbulence
mechanism.content: Stridor is a harsh, high-pitched sound caused by turbulent airflow through a significantly narrowed trachea or larynx. The obstruction creates audible noise during inspiration as air is forcefully drawn through the constricted upper airway.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a drinking straw that's partially crushed - the narrower it gets, the louder the slurping sound becomes as you try to suck liquid through the pinched section.

questions.q: A pleural friction rub sounds like:
questions.explain: Pleural friction rub = grating, creaking sound from inflamed pleural surfaces rubbing together. Heard in pleurisy.

optionExplains.text: Bubbling sounds are crackles from fluid in airways - not friction rub.
Correct! Friction rub = creaky leather, walking on snow. Two inflamed pleura scraping. Disappears if effusion develops (fluid separates surfaces).
Whistling is wheezing from narrowed airways - different mechanism.
Silence would be absent breath sounds (pneumothorax, effusion) - friction rub is an audible sound.

mechanism.title: Rubbing Inflamed Membranes
mechanism.content: When pleural inflammation roughens the normally smooth pleural surfaces, the visceral and parietal layers scrape against each other during breathing. This creates a grating, creaking sound synchronous with respiration that disappears if pleural effusion develops to separate the layers.
mechanism.metaphor: Like two pieces of sandpaper rubbing together - the roughened surfaces create friction noise with every movement until you pour oil (fluid) between them to lubricate the interface.

options: Increased clarity of spoken words over consolidated lung
questions.explain: Bronchophony = abnormally clear transmission of spoken words through consolidated lung tissue. Patient says 'ninety-nine' and it sounds unusually distinct.

optionExplains.text: Decreased breath sounds suggest effusion or pneumothorax - not bronchophony.
Correct! Bronchophony = voice sounds clearer than normal over consolidated lung. Consolidation transmits sound better than air-filled lung.
Normal voice transmission is muffled/indistinct. Bronchophony is ABnormally CLEAR.
Absent breath sounds suggest no air movement - bronchophony requires voice transmission.

mechanism.title: Transmitted Voice Clarity
mechanism.content: Bronchophony tests how clearly spoken sounds transmit through lung tissue. Consolidated lung (pneumonia) conducts sound better than normal air-filled lung, making whispered words unusually clear and loud through the stethoscope.
mechanism.metaphor: Like how you hear better through a solid wall versus air - dense lung tissue conducts sound waves more efficiently, amplifying the voice like a megaphone.

questions.explain: Egophony: patient says 'E' but examiner hears 'A' through stethoscope. Indicates lung consolidation or pleural effusion changing sound transmission.

optionExplains.text: Normal lung tissue transmits sound normally - E sounds like E.
Correct! E-to-A change = consolidated lung or effusion above the fluid line. 'E' transmitted as nasal 'A' (like a goat bleating - 'egophony').
Pneumothorax has DECREASED or absent breath sounds - not E-to-A change.
Asthma has wheezing and air trapping - not voice transmission changes.

mechanism.title: Voice Frequency Shift
mechanism.content: Egophony occurs when consolidated or compressed lung tissue selectively filters out lower voice frequencies while amplifying higher ones. When patients say 'E', the transmitted sound is heard as a nasal 'A' through the stethoscope due to this acoustic filtering.
mechanism.metaphor: Like talking through a kazoo - the instrument filters and transforms your voice by selectively amplifying certain frequencies and dampening others, changing the sound quality.

questions.explain: FEV1 = Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 second. How much air you can blow out in the first second of a forced breath. Key test for obstructive diseases.

optionExplains.text: Total lung capacity (TLC) is ALL air in lungs after max inspiration - different measurement.
Correct! FEV1 = volume exhaled in first second of forced expiration. Reduced in obstructive disease (asthma, COPD). Used with FVC for diagnosis.
Residual volume (RV) is air that CAN'T be exhaled - can't be measured by spirometry directly.
Tidal volume (TV) is normal breathing volume (~500 mL) - not forced expiration.

mechanism.title: One-Second Blast Volume
mechanism.content: FEV1 measures how much air you can forcefully exhale in the first second after a maximal inhalation. It reflects airway resistance and elastic recoil - obstructed airways reduce FEV1 because air can't escape quickly despite maximal effort.
mechanism.metaphor: Like squeezing a water bottle as hard as you can and measuring how much shoots out in one second - a narrow opening limits flow no matter how hard you squeeze.

questions.q: The FEV1/FVC ratio is reduced in:
questions.explain: FEV1/FVC ratio <70% (or <0.7) indicates OBSTRUCTIVE disease. Airway obstruction slows expiration, so less air exits in the first second.

optionExplains.text: Restrictive disease has NORMAL or INCREASED FEV1/FVC ratio - both volumes reduced but proportionally.
Correct! Obstructive = LOW FEV1/FVC. Airways are blocked, so air comes out slowly. FEV1 drops more than FVC. Classic for asthma, COPD.
Normal lungs have FEV1/FVC around 70-80% (age-dependent). Reduced ratio is abnormal.
Pregnancy mildly affects lung function but doesn't characteristically reduce FEV1/FVC.

mechanism.title: Obstruction Ratio Marker
mechanism.content: In obstructive diseases, airways narrow but total lung capacity often remains normal or increases. This reduces FEV1 more than FVC, lowering the FEV1/FVC ratio below 70%. Air gets trapped behind narrowed passages, taking longer to exhale completely.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a funnel with a clogged neck - the total container size (FVC) is normal, but only a trickle gets out quickly (FEV1), so the ratio of fast-to-total flow drops.

questions.q: In restrictive lung disease, both FEV1 and FVC are:
questions.explain: Restrictive disease (fibrosis, chest wall problems) = lungs can't expand fully. Both FEV1 and FVC reduced, but ratio stays normal or even increases.

optionExplains.text: Normal values would mean no disease. Restrictive disease has reduced volumes.
Correct! Restrictive = BOTH reduced, ratio NORMAL (or high). Lungs are stiff/small, so total capacity is down, but airways are open.
Increased volumes would be the opposite problem. Restrictive means REDUCED capacity.
FVC normal but FEV1 reduced is OBSTRUCTIVE pattern - the opposite of restrictive.

mechanism.title: Stiff Lung Constraint
mechanism.content: Restrictive diseases reduce lung compliance, making lungs harder to expand. Both FEV1 and FVC decrease proportionally, so the FEV1/FVC ratio remains normal or elevated. The lungs simply can't hold as much air, but what's there empties normally.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a smaller water balloon versus a clogged one - both the total capacity and one-second flow decrease together, keeping their ratio normal because the problem is size, not drainage speed.

questions.q: Peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) is most useful for monitoring:
questions.explain: PEFR tracks asthma control and response to treatment. It's a quick, portable measure of airway obstruction.

optionExplains.text: Pneumonia needs chest X-ray and labs - PEFR doesn't tell you about infection or infiltrates.
Correct! PEFR is the gold standard for home asthma monitoring. Patients track daily to detect worsening before symptoms hit.
Lung cancer needs imaging (CT, PET) and biopsy - airflow measurements can't detect tumors.
Pleural effusion needs imaging or ultrasound to see fluid - PEFR measures airflow, not fluid collections.

mechanism.title: Maximal Flow Speed
mechanism.content: Peak expiratory flow rate measures the fastest airflow velocity during a forced exhalation. It's effort-dependent and reflects large airway caliber - useful for monitoring asthma because bronchoconstriction immediately reduces peak flow even before symptoms appear.
mechanism.metaphor: Like measuring your car's top speed - it tells you if the engine (airways) is running freely or if something's restricting maximum performance, even if cruising seems fine.

questions.explain: SpO2 is a non-invasive estimate of arterial oxygen saturation using light absorption through the finger.

optionExplains.text: CO2 requires blood gas analysis (ABG) or capnography - pulse ox can't see CO2 at all.
Correct! Pulse ox uses red/infrared light to measure what percentage of hemoglobin carries oxygen. Normal is 95-100%.
Respiratory rate is counted by watching chest movement or using impedance - pulse ox doesn't measure breathing rate.
Lung volumes need spirometry - pulse ox only tells you saturation, not how much air you can move.

mechanism.title: Light-Based Oxygen Detector
mechanism.content: Pulse oximetry shines red and infrared light through tissue to measure oxygen saturation. Oxygenated hemoglobin absorbs different wavelengths than deoxygenated hemoglobin, and the ratio of transmitted light calculates SpO2 percentage non-invasively.
mechanism.metaphor: Like holding a flashlight behind your hand - red blood looks different than blue blood when light passes through, so the device can tell how much oxygen is loaded just by analyzing the color.

questions.explain: ABG provides comprehensive acid-base and oxygenation data from a single arterial sample.

optionExplains.text: Only oxygen would miss half the picture - you need CO2 and pH to understand acid-base status.
Correct! ABG gives you the whole package: pH (acid-base), PO2 (oxygenation), PCO2 (ventilation), HCO3 (metabolic compensation).
Only CO2 would miss oxygen and acid-base status - ABG is comprehensive, not limited to one gas.
Only pH would tell you acidotic vs alkalotic but not WHY or whether it's respiratory vs metabolic.

mechanism.title: Direct Blood Chemistry
mechanism.content: Arterial blood gas analysis directly measures pH, PaCO2, PaO2, and bicarbonate from arterial blood. This reveals respiratory function, acid-base balance, and oxygenation status simultaneously, providing the gold standard for assessing gas exchange and metabolic state.
mechanism.metaphor: Like taking a water sample directly from the source rather than trusting sensor readings - you get exact chemical concentrations instead of indirect estimates.

questions.explain: Retained CO2 forms carbonic acid (CO2 + H2O -> H2CO3), lowering blood pH.

optionExplains.text: Hyperventilation BLOWS OFF CO2, causing alkalosis, not acidosis. Opposite direction!
Correct! Hypoventilation = CO2 builds up = carbonic acid forms = pH drops. Think COPD, overdose, or weak respiratory muscles.
Vomiting causes METABOLIC alkalosis (losing stomach acid). This is a GI cause, not respiratory.
Diarrhea causes METABOLIC acidosis (losing bicarbonate). Also GI, not respiratory.

mechanism.title: CO2 Retention Acidosis
mechanism.content: Respiratory acidosis occurs when ventilation fails to eliminate CO2 adequately. Rising arterial CO2 combines with water to form carbonic acid, lowering blood pH. Causes include airway obstruction, respiratory depression, or lung disease impairing gas exchange.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a car running in a closed garage - the exhaust (CO2) builds up because it can't escape, making the air (blood) increasingly toxic and acidic.

questions.explain: Excessive CO2 loss from fast/deep breathing raises blood pH (less carbonic acid).

optionExplains.text: Hypoventilation RETAINS CO2, causing acidosis. This is the opposite mechanism.
Correct! Hyperventilation = CO2 blown off = less carbonic acid = pH rises. Causes: anxiety, fever, pain, high altitude.
Kidney failure causes METABOLIC acidosis (can't excrete acid or retain bicarb). Not respiratory.
Antacid overuse causes METABOLIC alkalosis (excess base). Not respiratory.

mechanism.title: CO2 Depletion Alkalosis
mechanism.content: Respiratory alkalosis results from hyperventilation that eliminates CO2 faster than it's produced. Decreased arterial CO2 reduces carbonic acid formation, raising blood pH. Common causes include anxiety, pain, hypoxia, or excessive mechanical ventilation.
mechanism.metaphor: Like over-ventilating a room in winter - you blow out so much warm air (CO2) that the environment becomes too cold (alkaline) for comfort.

questions.explain: Bronchial circulation is the NUTRITIVE supply - it feeds the lung tissue itself, not for gas exchange.

optionExplains.text: Alveolar gas exchange uses PULMONARY circulation (pulmonary arteries/veins), not bronchial.
Correct! Bronchial arteries branch from aorta and bring OXYGENATED blood to nourish bronchi, connective tissue, and visceral pleura.
Pulmonary veins are vessels, not tissue that needs blood supply. They carry blood, not receive it.
Heart muscle is supplied by CORONARY arteries, not bronchial. Different organ entirely.

mechanism.title: Lung Tissue Feeders
mechanism.content: Bronchial arteries branch from the aorta to supply oxygenated blood to lung tissue itself (airways, pleura, lymph nodes). This systemic circulation nourishes the structural components of the lungs, separate from pulmonary circulation that handles gas exchange.
mechanism.metaphor: Like the electrical wiring that powers a factory versus the assembly line inside - bronchial arteries keep the building (lung tissue) running, while pulmonary vessels do the actual product work (gas exchange).

questions.explain: Pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs for oxygenation.

optionExplains.text: Oxygenated blood LEAVES the lungs via pulmonary VEINS. The arteries bring UNoxygenated blood TO get oxygen.
Correct! The ONLY arteries that carry deoxygenated blood! Right ventricle pumps dark blood to lungs via pulmonary arteries.
Heart muscle is fed by coronary arteries from the aorta, not pulmonary arteries.
Lymph travels in lymphatic vessels, not blood vessels. Completely separate system.

mechanism.title: Deoxygenated Blood Delivery
mechanism.content: Pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs for gas exchange. Despite being arteries, they contain venous (oxygen-poor) blood - the only arteries in the body with this unique characteristic.
mechanism.metaphor: Like a delivery truck bringing dirty laundry to the cleaners - even though it's an 'artery' (truck making deliveries), it's carrying waste (deoxygenated blood) instead of fresh goods.

options: Oxygenated blood to the left atrium
questions.explain: Pulmonary veins return freshly oxygenated blood from lungs to the left atrium for systemic distribution.

optionExplains.text: Deoxygenated blood comes FROM the body via vena cava, not from the lungs. Lungs ADD oxygen.
Correct! The ONLY veins carrying oxygenated blood! Four pulmonary veins (2 from each lung) drain into left atrium.
Blood TO the lungs travels in pulmonary ARTERIES from the right ventricle. Veins carry blood AWAY from lungs.
Lymph travels in lymphatic vessels. Blood vessels carry blood, not lymph.

mechanism.title: Oxygenated Blood Return
mechanism.content: Pulmonary veins transport freshly oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left atrium. These are the only veins in the body carrying oxygen-rich blood, completing the pulmonary circulation loop before blood enters systemic circulation.
mechanism.metaphor: Like trucks returning from the cleaners with fresh laundry - even though they're 'veins' (return vehicles), they carry the cleaned product (oxygenated blood) back to be distributed.

options: All alveoli receive equal blood flow
Blood flow matches ventilation for efficient gas exchange
questions.explain: V/Q matching optimizes oxygen uptake and CO2 removal by directing blood flow to well-ventilated areas.

optionExplains.text: Equal blood flow would be wasteful - you want MORE blood going to well-ventilated areas, not equal distribution.
Correct! V/Q ratio ~0.8 is ideal. If an area is poorly ventilated, vasoconstriction redirects blood elsewhere. Matching = efficiency.
Respiratory rate is controlled by the brainstem based on CO2/pH, not by V/Q matching.
Lung expansion depends on compliance and pressure, not V/Q matching. These are separate concepts.

mechanism.title: Optimized Gas Exchange
mechanism.content: V/Q matching balances ventilation (airflow) with perfusion (blood flow) in each lung region. Gravity and local regulation ensure well-ventilated alveoli receive more blood flow while poorly ventilated areas get less, maximizing oxygen uptake efficiency.
mechanism.metaphor: Like routing delivery trucks to stores that have merchandise - it's wasteful to send trucks (blood) to empty warehouses (unventilated alveoli), so the system directs flow where goods (oxygen) are actually available.

options: Constricts vessels in poorly ventilated regions
questions.explain: This unique pulmonary reflex redirects blood away from poorly ventilated lung regions to optimize V/Q matching.

optionExplains.text: Dilation would INCREASE blood flow to hypoxic areas - wasteful! You'd be sending blood where it can't get oxygen.
Correct! ONLY in lungs: low O2 = constriction. This shunts blood to better-ventilated areas. Opposite of systemic vessels!
Bronchial arteries are systemic vessels - they DON'T have this hypoxic response. It's unique to pulmonary circulation.
Systemic vessels DILATE with hypoxia to bring MORE blood to oxygen-starved tissue. Lungs are the opposite!

mechanism.title: Local Oxygen Diversion
mechanism.content: When alveolar oxygen levels drop, nearby pulmonary arterioles constrict to redirect blood away from poorly ventilated regions toward better-oxygenated areas. This local reflex optimizes V/Q matching by reducing perfusion to hypoxic zones.
mechanism.metaphor: Like traffic automatically diverting from a congested highway exit - blood flow redirects away from oxygen-poor neighborhoods toward areas where oxygen loading is more efficient.

questions.explain: Atelectasis = alveoli that are collapsed or never expanded. From Greek: ateles (incomplete) + ektasis (expansion).

optionExplains.text: Lung cancer is a malignant tumor - different word, different pathology. Cancer = carcinoma.
Correct! Atelectasis = lung collapse. Causes: mucus plug (obstructive), tumor pressing (compressive), or lack of surfactant.
Lung infection is pneumonia. Atelectasis is mechanical collapse, not infection (though infection can follow).
Air in pleural space is PNEUMOTHORAX. Atelectasis is alveolar collapse, though pneumothorax CAN cause atelectasis.

mechanism.title: Alveolar Collapse Cascade
mechanism.content: Atelectasis occurs when alveoli collapse due to airway obstruction, external compression, or surfactant deficiency. Collapsed units can't participate in gas exchange, creating shunt (perfusion without ventilation) and reducing overall oxygenation.
mechanism.metaphor: Like empty grocery bags collapsing flat when not held open - without internal air pressure or structural support (surfactant), the delicate alveolar walls stick together and collapse.

questions.q: Infant respiratory distress syndrome is caused by:
questions.explain: Premature infants lack mature type II pneumocytes, so they can't make enough surfactant to prevent alveolar collapse.

optionExplains.text: Infection causes neonatal sepsis or pneumonia - different pathology. IRDS is specifically about surfactant.
Correct! Surfactant production matures around 35 weeks gestation. Preemies before this = high surface tension = alveolar collapse = IRDS.
Heart defects cause cyanosis and heart failure - different category of neonatal problems.
Meconium aspiration is a post-birth problem when baby inhales stool - causes chemical pneumonitis, not surfactant deficiency.

mechanism.title: Premature Surfactant Shortage
mechanism.content: Infant respiratory distress syndrome occurs when premature babies lack sufficient pulmonary surfactant, which normally reduces alveolar surface tension. Without surfactant, alveoli collapse on expiration and require enormous effort to re-expand, causing progressive atelectasis and hypoxemia.
mechanism.metaphor: Like trying to inflate a balloon coated with glue on the inside - the walls stick together and resist opening, requiring much more force than a normal balloon with slippery surfaces.

questions.explain: Hemoptysis = coughing up blood from the respiratory tract. From Greek: haima (blood) + ptysis (spitting).

optionExplains.text: Vomiting blood is HEMATEMESIS (haima + emesis). GI tract, not respiratory.
Correct! Hemoptysis = blood from lungs/airways, coughed up. Causes: TB, lung cancer, bronchiectasis, PE.
Blood in urine is HEMATURIA (haima + ouron). Urinary tract, not respiratory.
Nosebleed is EPISTAXIS. Upper airway but not from lungs - different source.

mechanism.title: Coughing Blood Source
mechanism.content: Hemoptysis indicates bleeding from the respiratory tract, ranging from bronchial irritation to serious causes like tuberculosis, cancer, or pulmonary embolism. Blood-streaked sputum suggests airway inflammation, while frank blood indicates vessel damage or erosion.
mechanism.metaphor: Like seeing red water coming from your faucet - it means there's a break or leak somewhere in the plumbing system upstream, requiring investigation to find the source.

questions.explain: Rhino- comes from Greek 'rhis/rhinos' meaning nose. Think rhinoceros - the nose-horned animal!

optionExplains.text: Throat is PHARYNGO- (pharynx). Different root, different anatomy.
Correct! Rhino- = nose. Rhinitis = nasal inflammation. Rhinorrhea = runny nose. Rhinoplasty = nose surgery.
Lung is PNEUMO- or PULMO-. Rhino- is exclusively nasal.
Chest is THORACO- or PECTORO-. Rhino- means nose specifically.

mechanism.title: Nose-Related Term Prefix
mechanism.content: The prefix 'rhino-' derives from Greek 'rhis' meaning nose. It appears in medical terms like rhinitis (nasal inflammation), rhinoplasty (nose surgery), and rhinorrhea (runny nose), identifying conditions or procedures involving nasal structures.
mechanism.metaphor: Like 'hydro-' always means water - when you see 'rhino-' at the start of a medical word, you immediately know it's talking about something nose-related.

questions.explain: Pharyngitis = inflammation of the pharynx (throat). The '-itis' suffix always means inflammation.

optionExplains.text: Nasal inflammation is RHINITIS (rhino- = nose). Pharyngo- = throat.
Correct! Pharyngitis = sore throat. Usually viral (cold) or bacterial (strep). Pharynx + -itis = throat inflammation.
Lung inflammation is PNEUMONITIS or PNEUMONIA. Pharynx is the throat, not lungs.
Bronchial inflammation is BRONCHITIS. Pharynx is above the trachea, different location.

mechanism.title: Inflamed Throat Lining
mechanism.content: Pharyngitis involves inflammation of the pharynx (throat), usually caused by viral or bacterial infection. Inflamed tissue becomes red, swollen, and painful, with increased mucus production and potential exudate formation on the pharyngeal walls.
mechanism.metaphor: Like the inside of your mouth after burning it with hot pizza - the tissue becomes red, swollen, painful, and produces extra saliva to try to heal the damage.

questions.explain: Laryngitis = inflammation of the larynx (voice box), causing hoarseness or voice loss.

optionExplains.text: Nose inflammation is rhinitis. Larynx is in the neck, not the nose.
Correct! Larynx = voice box. Laryngitis inflames the vocal cords -> hoarse voice or complete voice loss (aphonia).
Lung problems would be pneumonitis/pneumonia. Larynx is in the upper airway, not the lungs.
Bronchial inflammation is bronchitis. Larynx is above the trachea, which is above the bronchi.

mechanism.title: Vocal Cord Swelling
mechanism.content: Laryngitis inflames the larynx and vocal cords, causing swelling that interferes with normal vibration. This produces hoarseness or voice loss as the cords can't close properly or vibrate freely, plus cough from local irritation.
mechanism.metaphor: Like guitar strings that are wet and swollen - they can't vibrate cleanly to produce clear tones, resulting in muted, distorted sound or no sound at all.

questions.explain: Tracheostomy = creating an artificial airway by making a hole in the trachea. -stomy = creating an opening.

optionExplains.text: Throat surgery is vague - could be tonsillectomy, pharyngeal surgery, etc. Tracheostomy is specifically tracheal.
Correct! Tracheo- + -stomy = trachea + opening. Creates airway below obstruction (tumor, swelling, prolonged intubation).
Lung removal is PNEUMONECTOMY (pneumon- + -ectomy). Different organ, different suffix.
Bronchoscopy is LOOKING INTO bronchi (-scopy = viewing). Tracheostomy is CUTTING INTO trachea (-stomy = opening).

mechanism.title: Surgical Airway Access
mechanism.content: Tracheostomy creates a surgical opening directly into the trachea below the larynx. This bypasses upper airway obstruction, enables long-term mechanical ventilation, or facilitates secretion removal in patients who can't protect their airway naturally.
mechanism.metaphor: Like cutting a side door into a building when the front entrance is blocked - it provides direct access to the interior (lungs) without going through the obstructed path (mouth/throat).

options: Visual examination of airways with a scope
questions.explain: Bronchoscopy = using a scope to directly visualize the tracheobronchial tree. -scopy = looking/viewing.

optionExplains.text: X-ray of lungs is a chest radiograph or chest X-ray. Bronchoscopy is direct visualization, not imaging.
Correct! Broncho- + -scopy = bronchi + viewing. A flexible or rigid scope goes through mouth/nose to see airways directly.
Lung function test is SPIROMETRY or pulmonary function testing (PFT). Bronchoscopy is visualization, not measurement.
Blood tests include ABG, CBC, etc. Bronchoscopy is a procedure using a scope, not a lab test.

mechanism.title: Airway Camera Inspection
mechanism.content: Bronchoscopy involves inserting a flexible camera through the nose or mouth down into the trachea and bronchi. This allows direct visualization of airway anatomy, biopsy of suspicious lesions, removal of foreign bodies, or therapeutic interventions like tumor ablation.
mechanism.metaphor: Like using a plumbing snake with a camera - you can see exactly what's blocking the pipes, take samples of buildup, or even remove obstructions while looking directly at them.
